proposals.title: HALO: Hierarchical Abstraction Learning from Observation for Generalizable Robot Manipulation
proposals.motivation: Current robot learning methods for long-horizon tasks rely on manually designed task hierarchies, which are brittle, do not scale, and fail to capture the latent structure present in real-world demonstrations. This manual bottleneck limits the generalization and sample efficiency of learned policies, especially in open-world, user-driven scenarios. Automatically discovering this structure from data is a critical step toward robots that can flexibly acquire and reason about complex skills.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a neuro-symbolic framework, combining the pattern recognition of deep learning with the compositional generalization of symbolic reasoning, can infer a semantically meaningful and temporally consistent task hierarchy directly from unstructured demonstration data (video and action sequences).
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage framework. First, a transformer-based visual-language model (e.g., a fine-tuned ViT) will process demonstration videos to segment episodes into candidate sub-goals and generate natural language descriptions. Second, a probabilistic program induction method (inspired by Bayesian inverse planning) will operate on these noisy segments and language labels to infer the most likely hierarchical task graph, optimizing for temporal consistency and parsimony. This learned graph will then structure a hierarchical reinforcement learning policy for task execution.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, data-driven algorithm for inferring hierarchical task structures without manual specification, 2) a benchmark for evaluating learned hierarchies on real robot manipulation tasks, and 3) empirical evidence that such learned structures improve policy generalization and sample efficiency compared to flat or hand-designed hierarchies.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses fixed hierarchies or learns flat skill embeddings, our approach uniquely integrates visual-language perception with program induction to jointly recover both the temporal segmentation *and* the abstract symbolic description of the task hierarchy from raw demonstrations.

proposals.title: Graded Abstraction: A Transfer Curriculum for Learning Hierarchical Task Representations in Vision-Language-Action Models
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language-action models for robotics often struggle with the abstraction gap, failing to connect high-level natural language commands (e.g., 'tidy the room') to long-horizon sequences of low-level actions. This limits their ability to perform complex, multi-step manipulation tasks in real-world, human-centric environments. A principled method for learning hierarchical representations that bridge granularities is needed for robust and generalizable robot assistants.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a curriculum learning strategy, which progressively exposes a model to language commands of increasing abstraction during training, will induce transferable hierarchical task representations, leading to superior performance on novel, high-level instructions compared to standard training regimes.
proposals.method: We will develop a curriculum learning framework where a single transformer-based policy is trained on a mixture of robot manipulation datasets (e.g., RLBench, real-world teleop). The curriculum will be defined over the granularity of language commands, starting with dense, step-by-step instructions and gradually transitioning to sparse, high-level goals. We will employ a progressive neural network or elastic weight consolidation to mitigate catastrophic forgetting during curriculum phases. The training objective will combine behavior cloning on demonstrations and a temporal abstraction loss that encourages the model's intermediate representations to align with sub-task boundaries identified automatically from data.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel curriculum learning framework for hierarchical skill acquisition in robotics, 2) empirical evidence on how command granularity during training affects abstraction and generalization in VLMs, and 3) a new benchmark for evaluating hierarchical reasoning in embodied agents, measuring zero-shot performance on high-level commands unseen during training.
proposals.novelty: While curriculum learning is established, its application to structure the *abstraction level* of language supervision for inducing hierarchical representations in vision-language-action policies is novel. This directly probes the perception-reasoning interface in VLMs, moving beyond fixed command sets to learn a transferable abstraction model.

proposals.title: Frozen Instructors: Distilling Embodied Reasoning from Vision-Language Models into Robotic Feature Representations
proposals.motivation: Large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) possess rich world knowledge and reasoning capabilities, but are computationally expensive and not optimized for the specific perceptual and action-oriented needs of robotic control. Deploying them directly for real-time robot perception and planning is often infeasible. A more efficient, robotics-specific representation model is needed to leverage this knowledge for embodied tasks like manipulation and navigation.
proposals.hypothesis: Knowledge from a frozen, large-scale VLM can be effectively distilled into a smaller, task-specific visual encoder, and this distilled representation will enable more sample-efficient learning of robot policies and improve generalization on unseen tasks compared to training from scratch or using standard pre-trained visual backbones.
proposals.method: We will design a student encoder network (e.g., a compact ViT) and train it via distillation from a frozen teacher VLM (e.g., CLIP or a VLM like OpenFlamingo). The distillation objective will combine standard feature alignment on large-scale image-text data with a novel, robot-relevant auxiliary loss. This auxiliary loss will be computed on a curated dataset of robotic scenes (e.g., from RT-1, Bridge Data) and will enforce that the student's features preserve the teacher's ability to ground language instructions to visual states and predict actionable object affordances.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a publicly available, robotics-prioritized visual representation model that is efficient enough for real-time control. It will provide a framework for distilling high-level semantic and reasoning knowledge into embodied systems. We will empirically validate the representation's superiority in sample efficiency and generalization on benchmark manipulation tasks in simulation and on a real robot platform.
proposals.novelty: While model distillation is well-studied, distilling embodied reasoning and task-grounding capabilities from VLMs specifically for robotics is novel. The key innovation is the design of robot-centric distillation losses that move beyond simple feature matching to preserve actionable, instruction-relevant knowledge critical for policy learning.

proposals.title: LLM-Augmented Video Captioning for Scaling Robot Manipulation Policies from Human Demonstrations
proposals.motivation: Training robot manipulation policies from human video demonstrations requires dense, semantically rich captions to ground visual observations to language-conditioned skills. Manually annotating large-scale datasets is costly and limits scalability. Leveraging pre-2026 advances in large language models (LLMs) and vision-language models (VLMs) offers a promising path to automate this process, directly addressing the data bottleneck in embodied AI.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a framework using LLMs and VLMs to generate and refine descriptive captions for human activity videos can produce training data that enables learning more robust, generalizable, and interpretable language-conditioned manipulation policies than policies trained on sparse or template-based annotations.
proposals.method: We will develop a pipeline where a VLM (e.g., CLIP, BLIP-2) provides initial dense visual descriptions of video clips from human manipulation datasets (e.g., Something-Something, Ego4D). A large language model (e.g., GPT-4, Llama 2) will then be prompted to restructure these descriptions into hierarchical, task-oriented captions, incorporating object affordances and sub-goal reasoning. These augmented captions will be used to train a multi-task policy (e.g., a transformer-based architecture like RT-1 or ACT) for real-world manipulation, evaluated against baselines using original annotations.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, automated pipeline for generating high-quality, reasoning-infused captions for embodied datasets, 2) an empirical analysis of how caption quality and structure affect policy learning and generalization in manipulation tasks, and 3) a new benchmark dataset of LLM-augmented video captions for the robotics community.
proposals.novelty: While LLMs have been used for text augmentation, their application to generate structured, hierarchical captions for robot learning from videos is novel. The key innovation is the closed-loop evaluation of how these machine-generated captions directly impact the performance and robustness of learned real-world manipulation policies, probing the perception-reasoning interface for embodiment.

proposals.title: Synthetic Embodiment: Learning Generalizable Visuomotor Representations from Procedural Simulation and Perfect Captions
proposals.motivation: Scaling high-quality, diverse robot data is a fundamental bottleneck for learning generalizable visuomotor policies. Real-world data collection is expensive, slow, and lacks perfect annotations, while existing simulation data often lacks the semantic richness needed for complex reasoning and task planning. This work addresses the critical question of whether perfect, procedurally generated synthetic data can serve as a scalable foundation for embodied intelligence.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a visual representation pre-trained on a massive, procedurally generated dataset of simulated robot interactions, paired with perfect, dense captions describing actions, object states, and task progress, will yield features that transfer strongly and zero-shot to real-world robotic manipulation tasks, outperforming representations trained on internet-scale but non-embodied image-text data.
proposals.method: We will build a large-scale simulation framework in Isaac Sim to procedurally generate diverse manipulation tasks (e.g., pick-place, assembly, tool use) across varied scenes and objects. Each video frame will be automatically annotated with perfect captions detailing the action ("robot gripper moves left"), object state changes ("blue block is now on top of red block"), and task sub-goals. We will train a contrastive vision-language model (e.g., a CLIP variant) and a temporal video transformer on this data. The learned visual encoder will then be frozen and used as the perception backbone for training real-world manipulation policies via imitation or reinforcement learning, evaluating transfer performance on benchmarks like RLBench and real robot setups.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) A systematic study of embodiment-centric representation learning from synthetic data, quantifying the sim-to-real transfer gap for semantic visual features. 2) A publicly released large-scale synthetic dataset of captioned robot interaction videos. 3) Evidence for or against the sufficiency of perfect synthetic data as a pre-training resource for generalizable robot perception and reasoning.
proposals.novelty: While sim-to-real transfer is well-studied for low-level control, using perfect synthetic captions to train high-level, transferable visual representations for embodied reasoning is novel. It directly probes the 'perception-reasoning interface' for robots by isolating the role of perfectly aligned, task-grounded language supervision.

proposals.title: DIVERSE: A Scalable Benchmark for Evaluating Generalization in Embodied Multimodal Policies
proposals.motivation: Current robot learning benchmarks are narrow, focusing on a handful of tasks like tabletop manipulation or navigation, which fails to capture the breadth of real-world challenges. This limits our ability to measure and understand generalization, a core goal for useful, generalist robots. A unified evaluation platform with scaled task diversity is urgently needed to rigorously test the capabilities and failure modes of policies derived from large-scale models (e.g., VLMs, diffusion policies, RT-2).
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that systematically scaling the diversity of tasks in a unified evaluation suite will reveal critical, previously obscured gaps in the generalization of state-of-the-art embodied policies, particularly at the intersection of visual reasoning, language grounding, and long-horizon planning.
proposals.method: We will extend a platform like RLBench or Habitat 3.0 by integrating dozens of new, semantically distinct tasks across domains (e.g., articulated object manipulation, multi-room navigation with language instructions, social interaction proxemics, tool use). Each task will be defined by a formal POMDP specification, a procedural generation system for scene variation, and success metrics. We will develop a lightweight, modular API for policy evaluation and implement a suite of baseline agents, including fine-tuned VLMs (e.g., OpenVLA, RT-2), behavior cloning policies, and classical planners, to establish performance profiles.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the community with the first large-scale, open-source benchmark explicitly designed to measure generalization breadth in robot learning. It will produce a detailed taxonomy of failure modes for current methods and establish a new standard for evaluating progress towards generalist embodied agents.
proposals.novelty: While prior benchmarks add tasks incrementally, DIVERSE is designed from the ground up for scale and diversity, systematically covering a wider range of perceptual, reasoning, and manipulation challenges within a single framework to stress-test generalization, not just performance on known task distributions.

proposals.title: ELLA-Latent: Sample-Efficient Skill Composition from Self-Supervised Latent Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Enabling robots to learn complex, long-horizon manipulation tasks directly from raw visual observations remains a fundamental challenge due to the sample inefficiency of pixel-based reinforcement learning. While frameworks like ELLA enable efficient skill composition, they typically rely on engineered state representations, limiting their applicability in unstructured, real-world environments where such states are unavailable. This work bridges the gap between data-driven perception and structured reasoning for robot learning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by first learning a temporally coherent latent state representation via self-supervised dynamics modeling, the ELLA framework can be applied to this learned space to achieve sample-efficient skill acquisition and composition from pixels, outperforming both pure pixel-based RL and methods that lack explicit temporal abstraction.
proposals.method: We will train a recurrent latent dynamics model (e.g., a Variational Recurrent Neural Network or RSSM) on sequences of raw pixel observations using a self-supervised reconstruction and prediction loss. This model will produce a compact latent state vector. We will then adapt the ELLA (Efficient Lifelong Learning Agent) framework to operate over this learned latent space, using it to discover, acquire, and temporally compose skills (as options or skills in the MDP defined by the latent dynamics) to solve long-horizon tasks. The entire system will be trained end-to-end, with the latent model providing a stable, predictable state space for ELLA's symbolic planning and skill learning mechanisms.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a hybrid architecture that combines the representational power of deep, self-supervised visual models with the sample-efficient compositional reasoning of symbolic skill frameworks. It will demonstrate a path toward scalable robot learning from pixels by providing a principled method for temporal abstraction. We will validate the approach on simulated and real robot manipulation tasks requiring long-horizon reasoning.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the direct integration of a learned, temporally abstract latent dynamics model as the foundational state space for the ELLA framework, moving beyond engineered states or purely pixel-based policies to enable structured reasoning from raw visual data.

proposals.title: ELLA-V: Enabling Efficient Lifelong Learning from Raw Visual Observations via Hierarchical Feature Abstraction
proposals.motivation: Current robot lifelong learning frameworks, like Efficient Lifelong Learning (ELLA), often rely on pre-defined symbolic state representations, which are brittle and require significant engineering for each new environment. This limits a robot's ability to learn continuously from raw, high-dimensional sensory data, such as camera pixels, which is the primary input for real-world embodied agents. Bridging this gap is crucial for developing robots that can autonomously acquire and refine skills in complex, unstructured settings.
proposals.hypothesis: A neural architecture that learns hierarchical, task-relevant state abstractions directly from raw pixel observations can enable more flexible and data-efficient lifelong policy learning than methods dependent on hand-engineered symbolic states.
proposals.method: We will adapt the ELLA framework by replacing its symbolic state input with a learned visual encoder. This encoder will be a convolutional neural network (CNN) with a temporal component, inspired by hierarchical visual processing, to extract progressively abstract spatio-temporal features. We will integrate this with a probabilistic latent variable model to distill these features into a compact, task-relevant state representation for ELLA's multi-task policy library. The system will be evaluated in simulated robotic manipulation domains (e.g., Meta-World) and a real robot tabletop setting, comparing its sample efficiency and forward/backward transfer against symbolic-state ELLA and other visual RL baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, neurally-inspired lifelong learning architecture that operates directly on pixels, removing a major bottleneck for real-world deployment. It will provide empirical evidence on the trade-offs between learned visual representations and symbolic abstraction for continual learning. The resulting framework will advance towards robots that can learn new tasks from raw visual input without extensive perceptual re-engineering.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has combined deep RL with lifelong learning or used CNNs for perception, explicitly designing a hierarchical visual abstraction pathway to replace the symbolic state core of a proven lifelong learning framework like ELLA is a novel synthesis. This directly challenges the assumption that symbolic abstraction is necessary for efficient knowledge retention and transfer in continual learning.

proposals.title: Scaling Laws for Emergent State Abstraction in Pixel-Based World Models
proposals.motivation: Foundation models trained on internet-scale pixel data (e.g., video, egocentric streams) exhibit surprising reasoning capabilities, suggesting they internally learn structured world representations. However, there is no predictive theory explaining how and when such discrete, relational state abstractions emerge from purely sub-symbolic training. This gap hinders the principled design of scalable systems for embodied reasoning and long-horizon planning, which require robust state estimation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the emergence of abstract, compositional state representations in pixel-based foundation models is governed by predictable scaling laws, dependent on model capacity, dataset composition (particularly the prevalence of multi-view and multi-agent interaction sequences), and specific architectural inductive biases for temporal coherence.
proposals.method: We will train a family of transformer-based world models (e.g., variants of VideoGPT, Masked Autoencoders) on curated embodied datasets (e.g., Ego4D, MineRL) and synthetic environments, systematically scaling model size, dataset size, and temporal context. We will develop novel probing techniques, inspired by mechanistic interpretability and the cognitive paradigm from Karamcheti's prior work, to quantify the emergence of discrete, object-centric, and relational latent variables. We will analyze the learned representations using metrics for compositionality, causal invariance, and their utility for downstream policy learning in simulated manipulation tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirically grounded theory and quantitative scaling laws for emergent state abstraction in vision-based models. It will yield concrete architectural and data recommendations for building more sample-efficient and interpretable embodied AI systems. The resulting framework will unify perspectives on sub-symbolic learning and symbolic reasoning within a single scalable paradigm.
proposals.novelty: While prior work probes representations or studies scaling for next-token prediction, our proposal uniquely focuses on predicting the phase transition to abstract state spaces from pixels, linking scaling laws directly to the emergence of reasoning-relevant structure, a core challenge for embodied AI.

proposals.title: DROID-Ling: A Diagnostic Benchmark for Linking Linguistic Ambiguity to Robot Policy Failure Modes
proposals.motivation: As robots move from scripted labs to real-world human environments, they must follow ambiguous natural language instructions. Pre-2026, benchmarks like DROID measure task success but not the causal link between specific linguistic phenomena and failure. This gap prevents targeted improvements, leaving policies brittle to the underspecification inherent in human communication.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that robot policy failures on language-conditioned tasks are not random but systematically correlate with specific, identifiable categories of linguistic ambiguity, enabling a predictive failure taxonomy.
proposals.method: We will augment the DROID benchmark by programmatically generating and human-annotating instruction variations that introduce controlled ambiguities (e.g., vague references, missing steps, ambiguous pronouns). We will execute state-of-the-art vision-language-action policies (e.g., RT-2, PACT) on this augmented set, analyzing failure modes via structured logging of perceptual, reasoning, and action traces. This data will be used to construct a fine-grained taxonomy linking linguistic constructs to policy vulnerabilities.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce: 1) DROID-Ling, a public diagnostic benchmark for language-conditioned robot policies, 2) a validated taxonomy of failure modes linked to linguistic ambiguity, and 3) actionable insights for creating adversarial training curricula or modular uncertainty-resolution components to improve policy robustness.
proposals.novelty: While prior work probes VLM reasoning or measures overall policy robustness, this proposal uniquely operationalizes linguistic theory to create a causal, diagnostic link between specific ambiguity types and embodied policy failures, moving beyond correlation to mechanistic diagnosis.

proposals.title: CausalAdv: Adversarial Causal Disentanglement for Robust Vision-Language Grounding in Robotic Manipulation
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language models (VLMs) for robotic instruction grounding often rely on spurious correlations in training data, leading to brittle performance when faced with linguistic rephrasings or visual confounders in real-world settings. This fragility limits the reliability and safety of language-guided manipulation policies. A causally grounded, interpretable, and adversarially robust model is needed to enable trustworthy human-robot collaboration.
proposals.hypothesis: Enforcing a causal, disentangled representation of visual-linguistic factors (e.g., object attributes, spatial relations, actions) and adversarially training against generated linguistic and visual confounders will produce a more robust, interpretable, and generalizable model for grounding language in manipulation tasks.
proposals.method: We propose a framework where a large language model (e.g., GPT-4) generates diverse instruction hypotheses and plausible adversarial rephrasings. These are processed by a disentangled factor encoder that isolates latent factors into separate channels. A causal graph structure constrains factor interactions. An adversarial training loop uses a generator to create challenging visual confounders (e.g., distractor objects with correlated attributes) and linguistic perturbations, training the factor encoder to be invariant to them via a minimax objective, strengthening causal links.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel framework for causally disentangled vision-language grounding with built-in interpretability, 2) a methodology for generating and defending against multimodal adversarial examples in robotic contexts, and 3) empirical validation on real robot manipulation benchmarks showing improved robustness to linguistic and visual distribution shifts.
proposals.novelty: The integration of explicit causal structure within a disentangled VLM, combined with a closed-loop adversarial training regimen targeting both linguistic and visual domains, moves beyond correlation-based grounding and existing adversarial robustness techniques, which are typically unimodal and lack causal interpretability.

proposals.title: Bayesian Causal Inference for Robust Language-to-Policy Grounding in Manipulation
proposals.motivation: Current methods for grounding language in robot perception and policy, such as simple relevance classifiers or attention mechanisms, struggle with ambiguity, noisy sensor data, and novel instructions. They lack an explicit model of uncertainty and causal structure, making them brittle in real-world human-robot collaboration. This limits the deployment of robots in unstructured environments where instructions are underspecified or perceptual contexts are cluttered.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that framing language grounding as a hierarchical Bayesian causal inference problem—where the robot infers whether a language instruction and a perceptual state share a common latent task cause—will yield more robust, adaptive, and sample-efficient policies for real-world manipulation tasks.
proposals.method: We will develop a hierarchical Bayesian model with a latent variable representing the shared task cause. The model will take as input embeddings from a Vision-Language Model (VLM) for the visual stream and a language model for the instruction, estimating their respective uncertainties. Using variational inference, we will compute the posterior probability of a common cause, dynamically weighting the modalities. This probabilistic grounding will be integrated into a policy network (e.g., a diffusion policy or transformer-based architecture) trained via reinforcement learning in simulation and fine-tuned on a real robot manipulation platform, using datasets with ambiguous instructions and perceptual distractors.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel probabilistic framework for adaptive multimodal grounding that explicitly reasons about causality and reliability, 2) demonstrably more robust robot policies for language-conditioned manipulation in the presence of ambiguity and noise, and 3) a formal bridge between computational neuroscience models of perception and embodied AI.
proposals.novelty: While Bayesian methods exist in robotics for state estimation, applying hierarchical Bayesian causal inference—a model from multisensory integration in neuroscience—to the core language grounding problem for policy learning is a novel and non-obvious synthesis.

proposals.title: Curriculum-Guided Adversarial Pair Refinement for Robust Multi-Aspect Relevance in Embodied Instruction Following
proposals.motivation: In embodied AI, robots must retrieve and reason over visual observations and manual instructions to execute tasks. Current methods for learning relevance between queries and scene data rely on contrastive losses with noisy, coarsely labeled positive/negative pairs, which harms policy robustness. This is a critical bottleneck for reliable human-robot collaboration, as seen in ProVox's need for personalized, proactive planning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a curriculum-guided training process, augmented by an adversarial pair-refinement module, will yield more robust and granular multi-aspect scene representations, leading to improved performance on downstream manipulation tasks requiring complex reasoning.
proposals.method: We will adapt the CLIP contrastive framework for embodied settings, pairing language instructions with visual observations. A curriculum scheduler will initially train on high-confidence pairs (e.g., clearly relevant/irrelevant objects). An adversarial discriminator, inspired by GAN training but applied to pair quality, will continuously evaluate and down-weight ambiguous or potentially mislabeled pairs. Harder examples are introduced progressively, refining the model's ability to discern nuanced relevance for multi-step tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel training paradigm for contrastive learning in robotics that reduces sensitivity to dataset noise. It will produce a model capable of granular, multi-aspect relevance scoring, directly improving the perception-reasoning interface for VLMs in robots, as probed in prior work.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the integration of a learnable, adversarial pair-refinement mechanism within a structured curriculum for contrastive learning, moving beyond static datasets or simple hard negative mining to actively shape the training distribution for robustness.

proposals.title: CoReDiff: Contrastive Relevance-Guided Diffusion for Task-Aware Visual Scene Reconstruction
proposals.motivation: In embodied AI and human-robot collaboration, agents must reconstruct their visual environment not just for fidelity, but for task-relevant reasoning. Current methods treat high-fidelity reconstruction (e.g., via diffusion models) and semantic relevance (e.g., via contrastive VLMs) as separate stages, leading to reconstructions that may be visually plausible but misaligned with downstream task goals. A unified model that jointly optimizes for perceptual quality and task-specific utility is needed for efficient real-world robot policies.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a contrastive learning objective directly into the denoising training process of a latent diffusion model will force its internal representations to inherently encode semantic relevance, resulting in reconstructions with superior alignment to downstream task metrics without sacrificing visual coherence.
proposals.method: We will build upon a Latent Diffusion Model (LDM) architecture. During training, for each denoising step `t`, we will apply a contrastive loss (e.g., InfoNCE) on the intermediate latent features, contrasting positive image-text pairs (e.g., an image and its correct task instruction) against hard negative pairs (e.g., the same image with an irrelevant instruction). This loss will be combined with the standard diffusion denoising loss. We will evaluate on embodied datasets like RLBench or simulated kitchen environments, where 'relevance' is defined by alignment with a language-specified manipulation task.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel training framework for joint generative and discriminative learning in diffusion models, 2) a model that produces visual reconstructions optimized for task-specific reasoning benchmarks, and 3) empirical analysis of how contrastive guidance shapes the generative denoising trajectory, offering insights for embodied representation learning.
proposals.novelty: While contrastive learning guides representation space and diffusion models generate pixels, their deep integration during the iterative denoising process—where relevance signals directly modulate the generative refinement—is unexplored and non-trivial, moving beyond simple post-hoc filtering or classifier guidance.

proposals.title: BootCLIP: Bootstrapping Crowdsourced Curricula for High-Quality Embodied Instruction Tuning
proposals.motivation: Current instruction-tuning datasets for embodied agents are often static, lacking a principled progression in complexity, which can lead to poor sample efficiency and difficulty in learning long-horizon reasoning. Scaling high-quality data collection for robot task planning is bottlenecked by the need for expert annotators who can generate and validate complex, multi-step instructions grounded in visual scenes. A method to systematically train and assess annotators while generating data is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: A dynamic, curriculum-based crowdsourcing pipeline that adapts task complexity based on real-time annotator performance and consensus will produce higher-quality, complexity-grounded instruction-tuning data and simultaneously upskill the annotator pool, creating a scalable bootstrapping effect.
proposals.method: We will develop a web platform where annotators are presented with embodied instruction-completion tasks (e.g., 'describe the steps to make coffee' given a kitchen image) sequenced by a learned curriculum policy. Starting with simple object identification, tasks escalate to multi-step manipulation based on annotator accuracy and inter-annotator agreement (IAA). We will use a Bayesian model, akin to TrueSkill, to estimate annotator skill and task difficulty, dynamically adjusting the curriculum. High-consensus outputs from simpler tasks will seed templates and constraints for more complex ones, creating a self-improving data generation loop.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield: 1) a novel, scalable protocol for generating embodied instruction-tuning data with verified complexity progression, 2) the 'BootCLIP' dataset, annotated with difficulty and skill metrics, and 3) empirical evidence on how curriculum learning in data collection impacts the sample efficiency and reasoning capabilities of resulting Vision-Language-Action models.
proposals.novelty: While curriculum learning is established in model training, applying it *within* the data crowdsourcing pipeline to jointly train annotators and structure data generation is a novel synergy. This moves beyond static task lists or post-hoc filtering to an active, bootstrapping process.

proposals.title: Scaling Neuro-Symbolic Pretraining for Emergent Compositional Reasoning in Embodied Agents
proposals.motivation: Current large language models (LLMs) for robotics, such as those used in ProVox, exhibit brittle compositional reasoning, struggling to generalize novel task combinations. While neuro-symbolic methods can decompose tasks into sub-goals, they are typically applied post-hoc via fine-tuning, limiting their scope and scalability. Integrating structured reasoning directly into the foundation of a model is a critical, unsolved challenge for enabling robust, real-world human-robot collaboration.
proposals.hypothesis: Scaling neuro-symbolic instruction decomposition as a core pretraining objective on a diverse corpus of tasks and constraints will enable a model to learn a library of compositional primitives, and that this capability can be used to generate novel, valid training examples, creating a self-improving cycle for structured reasoning.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel pretraining framework for an LLM, augmenting its architecture with a differentiable neuro-symbolic module that explicitly represents task decompositions and logical constraints. The model will be trained on an ultra-diverse corpus of embodied tasks (e.g., from frameworks like BEHAVIOR or ALFRED), procedural text, and code, where data is annotated with sub-goal hierarchies. We will implement a training loop where the model's own decomposition outputs, filtered for validity, are used to synthesize new training data, creating a virtuous cycle of data and capability scaling.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a new class of foundation model with intrinsic, scalable compositional reasoning abilities, directly applicable to robot task and manipulation planning. It will provide a principled method for integrating symbolic constraints into neural pretraining at scale. The resulting model and open-sourced corpus will serve as a benchmark for evaluating emergent reasoning in embodied AI.
proposals.novelty: Moving neuro-symbolic decomposition from a fine-tuning add-on to a central pretraining objective is a significant paradigm shift. The key innovation is the closed-loop, self-improving data generation process, where the model's learned reasoning capability bootstraps its own training diversity, a concept not explored in pre-2026 neuro-symbolic or robotic foundation model research.

proposals.title: PlausiGraph: Learning the Instruction Manifold for Inverse Behavior Interpretation in Vision-Language-Action Tasks
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language-action models trained for instruction following struggle with the inverse problem: inferring the instruction from observed agent behavior. Existing methods treat this as a single-output regression or classification, ignoring the inherent one-to-many mapping where a single trajectory can result from multiple plausible instructions. This limits robust human-robot collaboration, as in ProVox, and hinders the automated generation of diverse, consistent training data for scaling embodied agents.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling the space of plausible instructions for a given trajectory as a structured manifold, and training a generative model to sample from it with verifiable consistency, will yield more robust and informative inverse interpretations than single-prediction models.
proposals.method: We will develop PlausiGraph, a two-stage framework. First, a conditional variational autoencoder (CVAE), conditioned on trajectory embeddings (visual and action sequences), will learn a latent manifold of plausible instructions. Second, a consistency verifier module—a fine-tuned vision-language model (e.g., Flamingo or BLIP-2)—will score and filter generated instructions for trajectory alignment. Training will use a combined loss: reconstruction loss for the CVAE, a contrastive loss from the verifier for consistency, and a diversity-promoting objective (e.g., based on determinantal point processes) to encourage coverage of the instruction space.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel generative framework for the inverse instruction-from-behavior problem, 2) a method for generating diverse, consistent instruction-trajectory pairs to augment training data for embodied agents, and 3) new metrics for evaluating the diversity and plausibility of inverse task interpretations, advancing the perception-reasoning interface studied in VLMs.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is reframing inverse interpretation as manifold learning and generation, moving beyond single-output discriminative models, and explicitly coupling a generative abducer with a learned consistency verifier to ensure semantic validity.

proposals.title: CORRECT: Scaling Robot Skill Acquisition via Large-Scale Pre-training on Simulated Language Corrections
proposals.motivation: Current robot learning paradigms struggle to efficiently acquire and generalize a broad repertoire of manipulation skills from limited real-world data. While large-scale pre-training has revolutionized vision and language, its application to embodied control, particularly for understanding and executing corrective feedback, remains underexplored. This work addresses the critical need for robots that can interpret open-ended human instructions and corrections to robustly perform long-horizon tasks in unstructured environments.
proposals.hypothesis: Pre-training a unified language-control model on a vast, procedurally generated dataset of simulated task executions and natural language corrections will enable the acquisition of a foundational correction-understanding capability, leading to significantly improved sample efficiency and generalization in downstream real-world manipulation tasks.
proposals.method: We will develop a simulation framework to generate millions of diverse manipulation episodes (e.g., in Isaac Gym) with paired failure modes and natural language corrections. A transformer-based policy architecture (e.g., a Decision Transformer or a state-conditioned diffusion policy) will be pre-trained on this dataset, learning to map multi-modal observations (images, proprioception) and correctional language to action sequences. The model will be fine-tuned on limited real-robot data (leveraging methods like residual policy learning) for tasks such as articulated object manipulation and proactive human-robot collaboration, as studied in ProVox.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a large-scale, open-source simulated dataset of language corrections for robot manipulation, 2) a pre-trained model demonstrating the 'embodiment scaling' benefits of correction-centric pre-training, and 3) empirical validation on real-robot tasks showing improved generalization and sample efficiency over task-specific or imitation-only baselines.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses language for task specification or offline RL datasets, we uniquely focus on pre-training for the *understanding of corrective feedback* as a core, generalizable skill. This bridges the 'perception-reasoning interface' probed in VLMs with embodied control, moving beyond passive instruction-following to active error correction.

proposals.title: Modular Assembly via Language (MAL): Training a Grounding LLM for Compositional Workflow Synthesis in Embodied Tasks
proposals.motivation: Current large models for robotics and vision-language tasks achieve broad generalization through massive scaling, which is computationally and data-intensive. A more efficient paradigm is needed where new capabilities emerge from the compositional reuse of specialized, pre-existing modules. This is critical for real-world embodied agents, where tasks are complex and require the flexible integration of perception, reasoning, and manipulation skills.
proposals.hypothesis: A central language model can be trained to function as a compositional 'grounding engine' that, given a novel instruction, synthesizes effective task plans by dynamically selecting and sequencing a fixed library of smaller, pre-trained functional modules (e.g., CLIP-based classifiers, code-generating LLMs, visual reasoners).
proposals.method: We will develop a hybrid architecture with a fixed library of modules (e.g., a visual affordance predictor, a spatial relation reasoner, a code generator for robot policies). A central BART or T5-based 'grounding model' will be trained via reinforcement learning and imitation learning on a synthetic dataset of compositional instructions. The reward function will incentivize correct task completion in simulation (e.g., AI2-THOR, BEHAVIOR) while penalizing inefficient module usage, teaching the model to correlate instruction semantics with novel, minimal workflows rather than retrieving monolithic solutions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a concrete framework and training methodology for learning compositional module assembly, 2) empirical evidence that capability breadth can be decoupled from single-model scale in embodied domains, and 3) a publicly released benchmark for evaluating compositional generalization in instruction-following agents.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior modular systems with hand-designed planners or retrieval-based module selection, we train a model to *learn* the mapping from language to novel compositional workflows, making the assembly process adaptive and grounded in task semantics. This directly challenges the scaling-law-centric approach to generalization.

proposals.title: Expert-FM: Training Narrow-Domain Foundation Models for Justifiable Multi-Modal Reasoning
proposals.motivation: General-purpose vision-language models (VLMs) lack the deep, nuanced understanding required for high-stakes domains like medicine or engineering, where answers must be justified by specific evidence. Current models often fail to identify and synthesize the most relevant snippets from complex, domain-specific documents containing text, figures, and tables. This gap limits their use in critical applications like diagnostic support or technical review, where interpretable reasoning is paramount.
proposals.hypothesis: A foundation model, pre-trained on a curated, narrow-domain corpus and fine-tuned for explicit evidence selection, will outperform generalist VLMs on domain-specific visual question answering by producing answers backed by a more accurate and interpretable chain of multi-modal evidence.
proposals.method: We will adapt a pre-2026 VLM architecture (e.g., Flamingo, BLIP-2) for domain specialization. First, we will continue pre-training on a curated dataset of domain documents (e.g., radiology reports with images). We will then fine-tune using a novel multi-modal chain-of-thought objective, training the model to output a sequence of evidence snippets (text spans, image regions, table cells) before a final answer. The training will leverage reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) to align evidence selection with domain-expert judgments of relevance and convincingness.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a framework for creating specialized, evidence-grounded foundation models and a benchmark for evaluating justifiable reasoning in narrow domains. It will advance the field of embodied AI and human-robot collaboration by providing a core reasoning module that can explain its decisions using domain artifacts, crucial for user trust in real-world manipulation and collaboration tasks.
proposals.novelty: While prior work probes VLM reasoning or personalizes models, this proposal uniquely combines narrow-domain foundation model training with an explicit, trainable evidence selection mechanism, moving beyond answer generation to the synthesis of an interpretable evidence chain as the primary output.

proposals.title: Expert-Foundation Models: Scaling Embodied Reasoning with Domain-Specialized Training
proposals.motivation: Current large-scale vision-language-action models (VLAs) are trained on broad, internet-scale data, which can lead to suboptimal performance on specific, high-stakes embodied tasks like precision manipulation or long-horizon household chores. This 'jack-of-all-trades' paradigm often fails to capture the nuanced physics, affordances, and failure modes critical for robust real-world robot policies. A focused investigation into the scaling laws of specialization, rather than generality, is needed for reliable robotics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that foundation models trained on curated, narrow-domain embodied data (e.g., dexterous manipulation, multi-room navigation) will achieve superior sample efficiency and final-task performance compared to broadly pre-trained models, when evaluated on in-domain expert tasks.
proposals.method: We will construct high-quality, narrow-domain datasets (e.g., 'kitchen manipulation') from real robot teleoperation and simulated demonstrations. We will then train transformer-based policy architectures from scratch on these datasets, systematically varying dataset size and diversity. Our core experiment will benchmark these 'Expert-Foundation Models' against a state-of-the-art broadly pre-trained VLA (e.g., RT-2) fine-tuned on the same data, measuring success rates, robustness to perturbations, and few-shot adaptation speed.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic study of scaling laws for embodied foundation models under a data specialization regime. It will yield concrete guidelines for when to train specialized models versus fine-tune general ones, and release high-quality, narrow-domain embodied datasets and model checkpoints to the community.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the dominant scaling hypothesis in embodied AI by prioritizing depth and quality of domain-specific data over breadth, directly testing the value of specialization for expert-level robotic performance—a question largely unexplored in pre-2026 VLA research.

proposals.title: Continual Embodied Learning via Dynamic Sparse Networks for Language-Guided Manipulation
proposals.motivation: Current robot learning paradigms rely on massive, static datasets and one-shot training, which is computationally expensive and brittle to new tasks or environments. Inspired by developmental neuroscience, where the brain learns continually via synaptic plasticity and pruning, we propose a more efficient and adaptive paradigm for embodied agents. This is critical for enabling robots to learn and reason over long-term, real-world interactions with users, a core challenge in pre-2026 robotics.
proposals.hypothesis: A neural network that dynamically prunes and grows sparse, task-specific pathways in response to new embodied experiences will achieve more efficient and robust continual learning than static, densely fine-tuned models, while preserving previously acquired skills.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework for a vision-language-action transformer, initialized as an over-parameterized network. As the robot encounters new manipulation tasks via human collaboration (e.g., from ProVox), we will apply biologically-inspired algorithms: (1) Synaptic importance scoring (based on weight magnitude and gradient flow) for iterative pruning, (2) Controlled neurogenesis via gradient-driven growth of new sparse connections for novel task features, and (3) Sparse experience replay to mitigate catastrophic forgetting. We will evaluate this on a continual benchmark of language-guided manipulation tasks in simulation and real robot setups.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel continual learning algorithm grounded in a neurobiological analogy, specifically tailored for the embodied, multimodal setting. It will provide a concrete framework for moving beyond static pretraining towards lifelong adaptive robots, directly advancing the scalability and personalization of human-robot collaboration.
proposals.novelty: While sparse training and continual learning exist, combining dynamic architectural growth/ pruning with embodied, multimodal (vision-language-action) learning is novel. The tight coupling of the neurobiological analogy with the practical demands of real-world robot task learning represents a significant departure from standard fine-tuning approaches.

proposals.title: Robust Affordance Acquisition: Adversarial Training for Interactive Foundation Models
proposals.motivation: Foundation models for robotics are moving beyond static datasets to learn object affordances through language grounding and real-world interaction, a paradigm shift exemplified by frameworks like RT-2 and VoxPoser. This emergent learning is vulnerable: poisoned language descriptions or adversarial visual patches could cause a robot to learn that a 'cup' is for 'throwing' or a 'knife' is 'safe to grip by the blade.' Pre-2026 adversarial robustness research focuses on classification, not on securing the dynamic, interactive process of affordance acquisition itself, creating a critical safety gap for embodied agents.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an adversarial training regimen, which uses the model's own interactive exploration in a simulated environment to generate and correct for affordance-poisoning attacks, will produce foundation policies that are significantly more robust to semantic and perceptual corruption while retaining their generalization capabilities.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage framework building on pre-2026 vision-language-action models. First, we train an adversarial attack generator to produce subtle corruptions in language instructions and object textures that maximally distort the affordance predictions of a base model (e.g., a VLM planner). Second, we employ adversarial training where the policy interacts in a high-fidelity simulation (e.g., Isaac Sim) with both clean and adversarially modified scenes. A key component is a verification module that flags inconsistencies between predicted affordances and physical interaction outcomes (e.g., expected vs. actual object stability), using these mismatches as self-supervised signals to update the model and the attack generator iteratively.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) A novel benchmark for evaluating adversarial robustness in interactive affordance learning, 2) A training framework that produces more secure and reliable embodied foundation models, and 3) New insights into the relationship between physical interaction and robust semantic grounding, advancing the field of safe human-robot collaboration.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work on adversarial examples for perception or language, we directly target and fortify the emergent affordance learning loop, using physical interaction as both a vulnerability probe and a corrective signal—a synergy not explored in pre-2026 robustness literature.

proposals.title: Stable Affordance Dynamics: Control-Theoretic Regularization for Open-Ended Embodied World Model Learning
proposals.motivation: Embodied agents must learn object affordances from open-ended language and interaction to operate in novel environments. Current methods, which often rely on static datasets or unconstrained online learning, suffer from instability, catastrophic forgetting, and misalignment between learned affordances and safe, effective policies. This limits their robustness and sample efficiency in long-horizon, real-world tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: By modeling the agent's affordance inference module as a dynamical system and applying control-theoretic stabilization, we can enforce stable, convergent learning from raw experience, leading to more robust and aligned downstream manipulation policies.
proposals.method: We will develop a dual-network architecture: a primary affordance inference network (a vision-language model) updated via interaction, and a lightweight meta-controller that models its parameter dynamics. Using techniques from Lyapunov stability analysis, we will design a feedback regularizer that penalizes updates causing large deviations in the output manifold or policy reward predictions. This controller will be integrated into a robot learning framework like RLBench or Habitat, using real robot data for manipulation tasks to provide a stabilizing loss during gradient-based updates from novel language descriptions and trial outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel framework for stabilizing open-ended embodied learning, a set of control-theoretic metrics for diagnosing learning instability in world models, and empirical evidence on real robot platforms showing improved sample efficiency and policy robustness in novel object manipulation tasks.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses regularization or meta-learning for stability, explicitly treating the world model learner as a dynamical system to be controlled with formal stability guarantees is a novel fusion of control theory and embodied AI. This moves beyond treating instability as a mere nuisance to be mitigated, instead framing it as a core control problem.

proposals.title: Cross-Modal Data Leakage: Auditory and Visual Membership Inference Attacks on Multimodal Foundation Models
proposals.motivation: Multimodal foundation models (e.g., CLIP, Flamingo, GPT-4V) are increasingly trained on vast, often private, datasets of paired images, audio, and text. As these models are deployed in embodied agents and collaborative robots, understanding their privacy risks is critical. Pre-2026 work on membership inference attacks (MIAs) has focused on unimodal models, leaving a gap in understanding how cross-modal alignment might amplify or mitigate the risk of inferring sensitive training data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the shared, semantically-aligned latent spaces of multimodal foundation models create stronger, more robust channels for membership inference and data reconstruction attacks compared to their unimodal counterparts, particularly for rare or distinctive data points.
proposals.method: We will develop novel MIA frameworks targeting models like CLAP (for audio-visual) and ImageBind. The approach will involve: 1) Crafting cross-modal query strategies, such as using a candidate image to probe for the likelihood of a paired audio clip in the training set via model confidence and gradient-based metrics. 2) Adapting and extending reconstruction attacks (e.g., from generative vision models) to attempt partial recovery of audio spectrograms or images from the multimodal model's internal activations. 3) Evaluating these attacks on standard robotic and human-activity datasets (e.g., Epic-Kitchens, Something-Something) to measure leakage strength.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic analysis of privacy risks in multimodal foundation models from a data inference perspective. It will deliver a benchmark for cross-modal MIAs and propose initial defense mechanisms, such as calibrated cross-modal contrastive loss, to enhance privacy for embodied AI systems that must process sensitive real-world sensory data.
proposals.novelty: While MIAs exist for vision and language models, attacking the *cross-modal linkages*—using one modality to infer membership of another—is unexplored. This directly probes the unique privacy implications of multimodal fusion, a core capability for next-generation robotic perception and reasoning.

proposals.title: Scaling Laws for Emergent Amodal Understanding: A Co-Designed Study of Multi-Modal Integration
proposals.motivation: Current large multimodal models (LMMs) primarily fuse vision and language, exhibiting impressive but brittle cross-modal abilities. For robust embodied reasoning and human-robot collaboration, agents must ground concepts in a unified representation accessible through diverse, often sparse sensory streams (e.g., haptics, force-torque, proprioception). The scaling principles governing the emergence of such amodal understanding from heterogeneous, multi-sensory data remain unknown, limiting progress towards generalist robotic perception.
proposals.hypothesis: Systematically scaling the diversity of integrated sensory modalities (beyond vision-language) and the volume of corresponding paired data will reveal new, predictable scaling laws for emergent cross-modal grounding and amodal reasoning capabilities.
proposals.method: We will construct a massive, novel dataset pairing core modalities (RGB, language instructions) with auxiliary robotic sensory streams (tactile arrays, proprioception, force-torque, audio) across diverse manipulation tasks. We will train a family of transformer-based architectures, inspired by Perceiver-like frameworks for modality fusion, while co-varying model scale, data volume, and modality count. We will rigorously evaluate emergent abilities through a suite of cross-modal inference probes (e.g., describing object material from haptics, predicting action from sound) to derive scaling laws.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first empirical scaling laws for amodal understanding in heterogeneous multi-modal systems. It will deliver a public benchmark for cross-modal grounding and a flexible architectural framework for integrating sparse sensory streams, directly informing the design of next-generation embodied AI agents.
proposals.novelty: While scaling laws exist for unimodal and vision-language models, this is the first systematic investigation of scaling with respect to sensory modality *diversity* for robotics, moving beyond simple input addition to study the emergence of a shared conceptual representation.

proposals.title: Evidence Chains for Embodied Reasoning: A Multi-Modal Selection Framework for Grounded Visual Question Answering
proposals.motivation: Current Vision-Language Models (VLMs) for Visual Question Answering (VQA) often generate answers by fusing all available image and text context, lacking the ability to explicitly identify and reason over the most relevant, task-specific evidence. This opacity hinders trust, interpretability, and performance in complex, real-world scenarios where information is redundant, contradictory, or distributed across modalities. Building on pre-2026 work probing the perception-reasoning interface, this project addresses the critical need for models that can actively select and justify evidence, a key capability for future human-robot collaboration and embodied AI systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a framework which explicitly models evidence selection as a sequential decision-making process, conditioned on the reasoning state, will produce more accurate, interpretable, and robust answers in multi-modal VQA than monolithic fusion approaches.
proposals.method: We will develop a neuro-symbolic framework where a trainable 'Selector' module, implemented as a policy network, sequentially queries a set of evidence sources (image regions, text snippets, table cells). This policy will be trained with reinforcement learning, using answer correctness as a reward, to learn to gather minimal, sufficient evidence. The selected evidence will be structured into a multi-modal 'evidence chain' and processed by a reasoning module, inspired by chain-of-thought methods, to generate the final answer. We will build upon and extend architectures like FILM for cross-modal alignment and ProVox's proactive planning for the selection policy.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel, interpretable framework for multi-modal evidence selection that bridges perception and reasoning, 2) a new benchmark dataset or task suite for evaluating evidence selection in VQA, and 3) empirical results demonstrating improved accuracy and robustness on complex, compositional VQA tasks, particularly those requiring negation or numerical reasoning from tables and images.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in framing multi-modal evidence selection as an embodied, sequential decision problem—akin to robotic manipulation of information—and in the integration of a trainable selection policy with structured, chain-based reasoning, moving beyond static attention mechanisms common in pre-2026 VLM architectures.

proposals.title: Decoupling for Robustness: Evaluating the Perception-Action Interface in Vision-Language Model-Based Robot Control
proposals.motivation: Current approaches to robot manipulation often rely on end-to-end visuomotor policies, which can be data-inefficient and fragile to distribution shifts in visual input. A promising alternative, leveraging pre-trained Vision-Language Models (VLMs) for high-level planning, is to decouple perception/planning from low-level control. However, the optimal interface between these modules and the necessity of end-to-end fine-tuning for robust action prediction remains an open question, critical for building reliable real-world systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for robust long-horizon task execution, a purely modular pipeline (frozen VLM planner → traditional controller) will fail on edge cases, and that a lightweight, end-to-end trainable interface between the modules is necessary to align the VLM's symbolic plans with the controller's geometric and dynamic constraints.
proposals.method: We will design a framework where a frozen VLM (e.g., GPT-4V) processes scene images and language instructions to output structured action plans. These plans will interface with a traditional controller (e.g., sampling-based or learned visuomotor policy) via a parameterized 'adapter' module. We will systematically compare: 1) a rigid, hand-crafted interface, 2) a trainable adapter with supervised learning on demonstration trajectories, and 3) end-to-end fine-tuning of the adapter via reinforcement learning in simulation (Isaac Gym) and real-world user studies. Metrics will include task success, robustness to perturbations, and plan-execution alignment.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous empirical evaluation of modular vs. end-to-end paradigms for VLM-based robot control. It will yield a publicly available framework for probing the perception-reasoning-action interface and demonstrate a method for efficiently grounding VLM reasoning in physical action constraints, advancing towards more sample-efficient and generalizable robot manipulation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses VLMs for planning or trains end-to-end policies, this proposal's novelty lies in its targeted investigation of the *interface* itself, treating the need for end-to-end training as a testable hypothesis rather than a presumption, directly building on Karamcheti's prior work on probing the perception-reasoning interface.

proposals.title: Architectural Scaling for Embodied VLAs: A Systematic Study of Base Models and Visual Encoders for Robot Manipulation
proposals.motivation: Current Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models for robotics, often built on Llama 2 and CLIP, show promising but brittle task performance. The field lacks a systematic understanding of how architectural choices in the base language model and visual encoder scale to embodied reasoning and real-world manipulation. This gap hinders progress toward robust, generalizable robot policies that can interpret complex visual scenes and follow nuanced language instructions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that architectural features from more recent language models (e.g., Mixtral's mixture-of-experts, Qwen's long context) and vision backbones (e.g., DINOv2's dense features, SigLIP's improved alignment) will yield VLAs with superior performance on metrics of reasoning accuracy, sample efficiency in policy learning, and generalization in real-world manipulation tasks.
proposals.method: We will construct a controlled VLA framework, adapting the architecture from recent work like RT-2, to test combinations of base models (e.g., Qwen-7B, Mixtral-8x7B, Gemma) and visual encoders (e.g., DINOv2, SigLIP, InternViT). We will train these variants on a unified dataset of robot trajectories (e.g., Open X-Embodiment) and evaluate them on a suite of benchmarks measuring visual grounding, language-conditioned planning, and closed-loop real-robot manipulation success. The study will isolate the contribution of each architectural component to final performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical scaling laws for VLA architectural components in an embodied context, offering concrete guidance for future model design. It will also release a set of benchmarked VLA checkpoints and a modular training framework to catalyze further research in efficient robot policy learning.
proposals.novelty: While prior work scales data or model size, we systematically vary the core architectural *types* of the constituent models in a VLA. This moves beyond the Llama/CLIP paradigm to identify inductive biases specifically beneficial for embodied, sequential decision-making, which is non-obvious from static vision-language task performance.

proposals.title: MORALE: A Multi-Object Reasoning and Language Embodiment Benchmark for Vision-Language-Action Models
proposals.motivation: Current benchmarks for Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, like CALVIN or LIBERO, primarily test sequential task completion in relatively sparse scenes. They lack the complexity of real-world environments with many distractor objects and rich, compositional language instructions that require complex visual grounding and relational reasoning. This gap limits our understanding of VLA generalization, a critical barrier to deploying robust robots in human-centric spaces.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that current VLA policies fail systematically on tasks requiring the compositional grounding of language to multiple objects in visually cluttered scenes, and that a benchmark isolating these factors will reveal scaling laws for embodied reasoning distinct from passive vision-language performance.
proposals.method: We will develop MORALE, a benchmark suite within high-fidelity simulation (Isaac Gym/PyBullet). It features procedurally generated tabletop scenes with 10-20 objects, where tasks are specified via templated and free-form natural language requiring object identification, comparison, and relational reasoning (e.g., 'stack the red block on the second-largest green cylinder'). We will evaluate a spectrum of pre-2026 VLA methods, including fine-tuned VLMs (e.g., RT-2, OpenVLA) and frameworks for policy learning from our prior work (ProVox), measuring success rates and analyzing failure modes across object count, language complexity, and visual clutter axes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) A publicly available, diagnostic benchmark for multi-object, language-rich embodied reasoning. 2) A systematic analysis of the perception-reasoning interface in VLAs under compositional stress. 3) Concrete scaling trends that inform the design of future embodied architectures and training datasets.
proposals.novelty: Unlike existing benchmarks, MORALE explicitly decouples and scales the complexity of visual clutter and linguistic compositionality, creating a controlled testbed to probe the interplay of perception, language grounding, and action that is central to real-world manipulation.

proposals.title: Scaling Laws for Embodied Visual-Language Models: A Study on OBELICS
proposals.motivation: Scaling laws have proven foundational in NLP and vision, predicting model performance as a function of compute, data, and parameters. However, their applicability to embodied AI, which integrates visual, linguistic, and action-oriented reasoning, remains unexplored. Establishing such laws for models trained on large-scale web-derived embodied datasets like OBELICS is crucial for efficiently allocating resources towards capable generalist robot policies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the performance of embodied visual-language models on downstream robotic reasoning and manipulation tasks will follow predictable power-law scaling with respect to model parameters and dataset size, with emergent capabilities appearing at specific scales.
proposals.method: We will train a series of transformer-based models (e.g., encoder-decoder architectures like Flamingo or unified sequence models) with parameters ranging from hundreds of millions to tens of billions, using the full OBELICS dataset of interleaved images, text, and action sequences. We will rigorously evaluate scaling trends on a curated benchmark of real-world robotic tasks, including language-conditioned manipulation and embodied VQA, measuring metrics like task success and plan correctness. The analysis will fit scaling laws to identify optimal scaling regimes and compute-performance trade-offs.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first empirical scaling laws for embodied visual-language models, providing a quantitative framework for forecasting the compute and data requirements for advanced robot intelligence. It will also release a family of scaled models and a benchmark to guide future research in efficient embodied AI development.
proposals.novelty: While scaling laws exist in pure language and vision domains, applying this rigorous empirical methodology to the multimodal, sequential decision-making space of embodied AI is novel. This work directly tests whether the 'bigger is better' paradigm holds for the complex perception-reasoning-action interface critical for robotics.

proposals.title: Scaling Interleaved Pre-training: A Unified Benchmark for Evaluating Multimodal Reasoning and Generation in Embodied Agents
proposals.motivation: Recent advances in Vision-Language Models (VLMs) trained on interleaved image-text data have shown promise for embodied reasoning, but their evaluation remains narrow, focusing on passive VQA or short-horizon robotic tasks. To guide the development of truly capable agents, we need a systematic understanding of how such pre-training scales across a broader spectrum of multimodal capabilities, from complex scene understanding and temporal reasoning to instruction-following and proactive task generation, all within a unified embodied context.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that models trained on large-scale interleaved multimodal data will exhibit positive, correlated scaling laws not just on standard visual QA, but across a diverse suite of embodied reasoning and generation tasks, with performance gains being most pronounced on tasks requiring tight perception-reasoning-action integration.
proposals.method: We will construct a comprehensive benchmark, 'Embodied Multimodal Task Suite (EMTS)', integrating and extending existing datasets (e.g., ALFRED, Ego4D, VQA-v2, Something-Something) into a unified framework centered on robot manipulation and human collaboration. We will evaluate a range of pre-2026 model families (e.g., Flamingo, GATO-2, RT-2) trained on interleaved data, using a consistent evaluation protocol. The core technical approach involves adapting these models via parameter-efficient fine-tuning (e.g., LoRA) on our benchmark's training splits and rigorously measuring performance across task categories: visual reasoning, temporal forecasting, language-conditioned policy learning, and proactive plan generation, as in ProVox.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic scaling analysis of interleaved pre-training for embodied AI, yielding concrete scaling curves across diverse multimodal capabilities. It will produce a public benchmark (EMTS) to drive future research and offer insights into which pre-training data mixtures and model architectures best transfer to complex, real-world robotic tasks requiring deep perception-reasoning interfaces.
proposals.novelty: While prior work benchmarks VLMs on static images or robots on narrow skills, our proposal uniquely evaluates the *correlated scaling* of interleaved models across a unified spectrum of reasoning and generation tasks directly relevant to embodied agents, probing the transfer between internet-scale pre-training and situated, real-world performance.

proposals.title: Open-World Scene-Action Graphs: Grounding Open-Vocabulary Task Instructions for Robotic Manipulation
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, robots trained for manipulation struggle with novel objects and scene descriptions absent from their training data, limiting real-world deployment. While large vision-language models (VLMs) offer rich open-vocabulary scene understanding, grounding this knowledge into actionable, sequential robot policies for long-horizon tasks remains an open challenge. This work addresses the critical gap between high-level semantic perception and low-level, reliable policy execution in unstructured environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a neuro-symbolic framework, which constructs a dynamic Scene-Action Graph from VLM outputs and uses it to guide a learned skill policy library, will enable robust generalization to open-vocabulary object and scene terms for long-horizon manipulation tasks.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-tiered architecture. First, a VLM (e.g., OFA or Flamingo) will process egocentric video and a natural language instruction to generate an initial symbolic Scene-Action Graph, representing objects, their affordances, and task-relevant relations. This graph will be continuously updated via a lightweight, fine-tuned VLM module that performs visual reasoning to verify state and handle ambiguities. Second, a pre-trained manipulation policy library, conditioned on graph nodes and embeddings, will execute parameterized skills (e.g., grasping, placing). We will train and evaluate in simulation (e.g., RLBench, Habitat) and perform real-world validation on a mobile manipulator for tasks involving novel object categories and compositional scene descriptions.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel neuro-symbolic framework for translating open-vocabulary instructions into executable robot action sequences, 2) a benchmark for evaluating compositional generalization in instruction-following for manipulation, and 3) empirical results demonstrating improved sample efficiency and generalization over end-to-end VLM-to-policy baselines in open-world settings.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tight, closed-loop integration of a dynamically updated symbolic graph (derived from a VLM) with a learned skill policy, moving beyond using VLMs as static perception modules or for one-step affordance prediction, towards proactive reasoning for multi-step tasks.

proposals.title: Contrastive Action-Language Alignment (CALA): Grounding Language in Latent Action Geometry for Robotic Manipulation
proposals.motivation: In vision-language-action models for robotics, a critical gap exists between high-level language instructions and the low-dimensional geometry of the latent action space learned by policies. Pre-2026 methods often rely on simple projection layers, leading to misalignment where semantically similar instructions (e.g., 'push the mug left' vs. 'nudge the cup sideways') map to distant action embeddings, impairing generalization and user interaction. This problem is acute in real-world, user-in-the-loop manipulation tasks where instruction variability is high.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly structuring the language embedding space to reflect the geometric relationships within a pre-trained latent action space via contrastive learning will significantly improve the robustness and generalization of language-conditioned robotic policies for manipulation.
proposals.method: We will develop the Contrastive Action-Language Alignment (CALA) framework. A frozen visual encoder and a pre-trained latent action policy (e.g., a diffusion or VAE-based model) provide the geometric anchor. We will train a language encoder using a multi-modal contrastive loss (e.g., InfoNCE) on paired (observation, language instruction, action) data. Crucially, the loss will incorporate not just positive pairs but also hard negatives generated by sampling actions within the latent space that are geometrically proximate but semantically distinct, forcing the language model to respect the action manifold's structure. We will validate on real-robot manipulation benchmarks and within the ProVox proactive planning framework.
