proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel contrastive learning objective for geometrically consistent language grounding, 2) demonstrably improved sample efficiency and instruction-following accuracy on diverse manipulation tasks, and 3) a formal analysis of the perception-reasoning interface in VLMs as probed through the geometry of their aligned spaces, extending the researcher's prior cognitive paradigm work.
proposals.novelty: While contrastive learning is established in vision-language models (e.g., CLIP), its application to explicitly align natural language with the intrinsic geometry of a latent *action* space for robotics is novel. This moves beyond typical task-level alignment to ground language in the fundamental control manifold, a non-obvious step given existing work that treats language and action embeddings separately.

proposals.title: AutoAnnotate: Scaling Robot Policy Learning with Foundation Model-Generated Dense Trajectory Annotations
proposals.motivation: Learning robust, generalizable robot manipulation policies from demonstration requires large, diverse, and richly annotated datasets. However, manual annotation of robot trajectories with dense language descriptions or sub-task labels is prohibitively expensive and unscalable. This bottleneck severely limits the data efficiency and semantic grounding of learned policies, particularly for long-horizon, multi-step tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: Large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) like GPT-4V, when prompted with structured reasoning frameworks, can generate accurate, temporally dense, and semantically meaningful annotations for robot manipulation trajectories, and these automated annotations can be used to train more data-efficient and interpretable robot policies.
proposals.method: We will develop a pipeline that processes egocentric video and proprioceptive state data from robot trajectories. We will adapt the cognitive paradigm probing from Karamcheti's prior work to design VLM prompts that decompose trajectories into sub-tasks and generate step-by-step language descriptions. We will integrate this with a proactive planning module (inspired by ProVox) to infer and label the robot's intended sub-goals. The generated annotations will be used to train hierarchical policies or to condition diffusion policies, evaluating their performance against policies trained on human-labeled data.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a scalable, automated method for generating high-quality robot trajectory annotations, 2) a new benchmark dataset of VLM-annotated manipulation trajectories, and 3) an empirical analysis of how such annotations impact policy learning efficiency and generalization in real-world manipulation tasks.
proposals.novelty: While VLMs have been used for single-image captioning, their application to dense, temporal annotation of robot trajectories—integrating visual reasoning with inferred intent for policy learning—is a novel direction. This moves beyond using VLMs for passive scene description to active, task-centric annotation for robot learning.

proposals.title: Video-PreTrained Policies: Transferring Web-Scale Spatiotemporal Representations to Real-World Robot Manipulation
proposals.motivation: Current robot learning methods often train visual representations from scratch on limited, domain-specific robot data, which is inefficient and struggles with generalization. Large-scale web video datasets (e.g., Ego4D, Something-Something) contain rich, diverse depictions of physical interactions and object affordances, offering a potential foundation for more robust and data-efficient robot perception. This work investigates whether pre-training on such passive video can significantly improve policy learning for real-world manipulation tasks, addressing a core challenge in scaling embodied AI.
proposals.hypothesis: Pre-training a visual backbone on large-scale, egocentric web video data using self-supervised objectives for spatiotemporal reasoning will yield representations that, when fine-tuned on the DROID robot dataset, produce policies with superior sample efficiency, generalization to novel objects, and robustness to visual distractions compared to training from scratch or using static-image pre-training.
proposals.method: We will adopt a Vision Transformer (ViT) or a Video Swin Transformer as the visual encoder. It will be pre-trained on the Ego4D dataset using a masked autoencoding (MAE) objective extended to video, learning to reconstruct spatiotemporal patches. This pre-trained encoder will then be frozen or lightly fine-tuned and integrated into an action prediction policy (e.g., a diffusion policy or transformer-based policy) trained on the DROID dataset. We will benchmark against baselines using ImageNet-pre-trained and randomly initialized encoders, evaluating on DROID's real-world manipulation tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous empirical study on the value of web video pre-training for robot manipulation, establishing new benchmarks for sample efficiency and generalization. It will contribute a publicly released pre-trained visual model optimized for embodied tasks and offer insights into which spatiotemporal pre-training objectives are most transferable to action.
proposals.novelty: While video pre-training is known for action recognition, its systematic application and evaluation for closed-loop robot policy learning, particularly with a focus on real-world fine-tuning and generalization in manipulation, is underexplored. This bridges the gap between large-scale passive video understanding and actionable, task-oriented representation learning for robots.

proposals.title: Inverse Task Inference: Recovering Task Descriptions from Robot Interaction Videos via Spatio-Temporal Reasoning
proposals.motivation: Current robot learning paradigms rely on pre-defined task descriptions or human instructions to guide policy learning. However, for robots to learn from vast, unlabeled interaction data (e.g., from the web or passive observation), they must solve the inverse problem: inferring the intended task goal from visual demonstrations. This capability is crucial for scalable, data-driven robot learning and for enabling more natural human-robot collaboration where the robot must understand implicit human intent.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by jointly modeling the spatio-temporal structure of object interactions and leveraging the semantic grounding of large vision-language models (VLMs), a model can reliably infer a structured, language-based task description from a raw robot interaction video.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage architecture. First, a video encoder (e.g., a pre-trained VideoMAE or TimeSformer) will extract spatio-temporal features. These features will condition a large language model (LLM) via cross-attention, framing the problem as conditional text generation. To ground the inference, we will integrate object-centric representations (e.g., from DETR or Slot Attention) into the encoder to track object state changes. The model will be trained on existing robot datasets (e.g., Bridge, LIBERO, or Ego4D) paired with natural language instructions, treating the video as input and the task description as the target output.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel benchmark and model for the inverse task inference problem, 2) a methodology for grounding high-level task descriptions in low-level visual interaction sequences, and 3) a tool for automatically annotating large-scale robot video data with task labels, facilitating future policy learning.
proposals.novelty: While VLMs excel at describing scenes, our focus on inferring the *intended task goal* from a robot's *interaction history* moves beyond passive captioning to active reasoning about agent-object affordances and subgoal completion, a distinct challenge from standard video captioning.

proposals.title: Curriculum-VLM: A Self-Supervised Visual Warm-Start for Data-Efficient Joint Vision-Language Training
proposals.motivation: Training Vision-Language Models (VLMs) from scratch requires massive, expensive aligned datasets of image-text pairs, creating a significant bottleneck for specialized domains like robotics. While pre-training on large static datasets like ImageNet is common, it can limit adaptability and lead to domain mismatch. A method is needed to efficiently learn general visual representations that can be effectively aligned with language for downstream reasoning and manipulation tasks without relying on vast curated datasets.
proposals.hypothesis: A curriculum learning approach, where a visual encoder is first warmed up via self-supervision on unlabeled images and then jointly fine-tuned with a language model on a smaller aligned dataset, will yield a performant, adaptable VLM with significantly reduced data requirements compared to pure joint-from-scratch training.
proposals.method: We propose a two-phase framework. Phase 1: A visual encoder (e.g., ViT) is trained from scratch using a self-supervised objective like DINO or iBOT on a large, domain-relevant collection of unlabeled images (e.g., robotic egocentric videos). Phase 2: This pre-trained, non-frozen encoder is integrated with a language model (e.g., a decoder-only transformer) via a cross-attention mechanism. The entire model is then jointly fine-tuned on a curated vision-language dataset for a target task (e.g., instruction following for manipulation), allowing the visual features to specialize while maintaining alignment flexibility.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete recipe for data-efficient VLM training, reducing reliance on massive aligned corpora. It will demonstrate the value of self-supervised visual curricula for embodied AI, leading to more adaptable models for robot perception and reasoning. The resulting framework will be a step towards 'embodiment scaling laws' where visual foundation quality is a key variable.
proposals.novelty: Unlike standard practice of using a fixed, ImageNet-pre-trained encoder, our visual encoder is trained from scratch on task-relevant data and remains adaptable during joint training. This bridges the gap between the flexibility of joint-from-scratch training and the data efficiency of transfer learning, a non-trivial combination for embodied domains.

proposals.title: JOVIAL: Jointly-Optimized Vision-and-Language Models for Embodied Task Reasoning
proposals.motivation: Current Vision-Language Models (VLMs) for robotics rely on frozen, pretrained visual encoders (e.g., CLIP) that are optimized for static, web-scale image-text alignment. This creates a fundamental misalignment for embodied tasks, where visual features must support sequential decision-making, spatial reasoning, and manipulation affordances. A frozen encoder cannot adapt its visual representations to the specific demands of robot policies and task-oriented reasoning.
proposals.hypothesis: A VLM trained from scratch with a jointly-learned visual encoder, using embodied interaction data, will develop visual representations more conducive to downstream robot reasoning and policy learning than a model using a frozen, generic visual encoder.
proposals.method: We will design a transformer-based architecture where a randomly initialized Vision Transformer (ViT) patch encoder is directly connected to a language model backbone (e.g., a pretrained LLM like Llama-2). The entire model will be trained end-to-end on a multimodal corpus of robot data, including egocentric video, language instructions, and action sequences (e.g., from datasets like RT-1, Open X-Embodiment). We will employ a masked autoencoding objective on visual patches combined with a next-token prediction loss on language and discretized actions, forcing the visual encoder to learn features predictive of both scene semantics and potential robot behaviors.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic evidence for or against the necessity of frozen visual encoders in embodied VLMs. It will produce a new model architecture and training paradigm for robot learning that tightly couples visual perception with action-oriented reasoning. The resulting model and analysis will inform future scaling laws for embodied AI.
proposals.novelty: While prior work fine-tunes adapters on frozen visual features, we challenge this core architectural assumption by learning visual features from scratch, directly optimized for the embodied reasoning loop. This departs from the standard 'pretrain-then-freeze' VLM paradigm dominant in pre-2026 research.

proposals.title: Crystallization Curriculum: A Three-Phase Training Framework for Robust and Knowledgeable Vision-Language Models
proposals.motivation: Current VLM training relies heavily on massive, noisy web-scale datasets, which can embed harmful biases, reasoning errors, and poor multimodal grounding. While curation improves quality, it limits knowledge breadth. There is a critical need for training paradigms that combine the reliability of curated data with the expansive coverage of web data, especially for embodied agents where safety and robust reasoning are paramount.
proposals.hypothesis: A curriculum that first establishes robust reasoning and safety on a small, high-quality 'seed' dataset, then strategically expands knowledge via a learned data router, will produce VLMs that outperform models trained on static mixtures in both reliability and knowledge coverage.
proposals.method: We propose a three-phase framework: 1) Seed Training: Train a VLM from scratch on a small, curated dataset (e.g., a subset of high-quality instructional or embodied interaction data). 2) Expansion Phase: Employ a lightweight routing network (e.g., a Perceiver-based scorer) to dynamically select batches from a massive, noisy corpus (e.g., LAION) based on utility (predicted improvement on a knowledge probe) and compatibility (similarity to the seed data's embedding distribution). 3) Crystallization Phase: Fine-tune the model on a blended high-quality set to reinforce alignment. We will evaluate on embodied reasoning benchmarks (e.g., VIMA, BEHAVIOR) and adversarial robustness probes.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, scalable training curriculum for VLMs and a concrete method for intelligent data selection. It will provide empirical evidence on the interplay between data quality and quantity in VLM training, offering a path toward more reliable models for human-robot collaboration.
proposals.novelty: Unlike static dataset mixing or simple filtering, our method introduces a *learned, dynamic routing mechanism* that actively shapes the training curriculum based on the model's evolving needs, blending principles from curriculum learning and data selection for large-scale training.

proposals.title: Genome-Inspired Vision-Language Pre-training: A Reference Corpus Protocol for Robust Embodied Reasoning
proposals.motivation: Current Vision-Language Models (VLMs) are pre-trained on massive, noisy web-scraped datasets, which can embed spurious correlations and misaligned representations that hinder robust reasoning. This is particularly critical for embodied AI and robotics, where models must perform precise, grounded reasoning for manipulation and collaboration tasks. A new paradigm is needed to build more reliable, generalizable, and interpretable multi-modal representations from the outset.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that initializing VLM pre-training with a small, meticulously curated 'reference corpus' of perfectly aligned vision-language exemplars will create a more robust foundational representation, which will then act as a stabilizing template, improving learning efficiency and final performance when scaling to noisy, large-scale data.
proposals.method: We will construct a curated 'reference genome' corpus focusing on core physical concepts, object affordances, and spatio-temporal relationships critical for robotics. Using a standard VLM architecture (e.g., a ViT-L/14 vision encoder with a LLM like Llama-3), we will pre-train it to convergence on this corpus. Subsequently, we will employ a constrained fine-tuning or gated adapter-based method (e.g., LoRA) when training on large-scale datasets like LAION, where the reference model's representations guide parameter updates to prevent catastrophic forgetting of core concepts. We will evaluate on embodied reasoning benchmarks (e.g., VLN, ALFRED) and robot manipulation policies in simulation.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, biologically-inspired pre-training protocol for VLMs, 2) a publicly released curated reference corpus for embodied reasoning, and 3) empirical evidence on how high-quality data prioritization affects scaling laws and the robustness of learned representations for downstream robotic tasks.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the dominant 'scale-first' pre-training paradigm by prioritizing data quality and curation at the initial stage, drawing a direct analogy from genomics. It proposes using the resulting model as a stable template to filter noisy data, a method distinct from simple curriculum learning or data filtering.

proposals.title: Quality over Quantity: Curated Embodied Data for Robust Robot Visual-Language-Action Policies
proposals.motivation: Current Vision-Language Models (VLMs) for robotics are often pre-trained on massive, noisy web-scraped datasets (e.g., LAION), which embed biases and lack the precise, physically-grounded reasoning required for real-world manipulation. This misalignment leads to fragile policies that fail under distribution shift in user-driven, interactive tasks. A paradigm shift is needed to prioritize data quality and task-relevance over sheer scale.
proposals.hypothesis: A VLM fine-tuned on a small, meticulously curated dataset of multi-view video, language instructions, and action trajectories will outperform a model pre-trained on orders-of-magnitude more generic web data on metrics of task success, reasoning coherence, and user instruction following in novel manipulation scenarios.
proposals.method: We will construct 'RoboCurate,' a dataset of ~10k high-fidelity manipulation episodes in diverse real environments, each with multi-view video, structured language annotations (inspired by ProVox's proactive planning), and precise action sequences. Using a pre-trained VLM (e.g., OpenFlamingo) as a base, we will employ instruction tuning and behavior cloning loss to train an end-to-end policy. We will rigorously compare against a baseline model trained on a scaled-down version of a standard web dataset (e.g., CC12M), controlling for compute, using the paradigm from our cognitive probing work to evaluate the perception-reasoning interface.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a proof-of-concept that data curation can surpass scaling for embodied AI, 2) the publicly released 'RoboCurate' benchmark for evaluating grounded reasoning, and 3) a framework for building efficient, user-aligned robot policies that are more sample-efficient and interpretable than current large-scale approaches.
proposals.novelty: While data curation is known, its systematic prioritization over web-scale pre-training for embodied VLMs is unexplored. This inverts the core assumption driving field progress and directly connects high-quality data to improved cognitive reasoning and task performance, not just visual recognition.

proposals.title: Probing the Instruction-Execution Gap: A Diagnostic Framework for Decomposing VLM Competence
proposals.motivation: Instruction tuning is a dominant paradigm for aligning Vision-Language Models (VLMs) with human intent, but it conflates learning task semantics with learning to parse specific linguistic formats. This conflation obscures whether performance failures stem from a lack of core reasoning competence or merely a misalignment with the instruction's surface form. A rigorous diagnostic is needed to guide more efficient, targeted training for embodied AI and human-robot collaboration, where robustness to varied, real-world user commands is critical.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant portion of the performance gap in instruction-tuned VLMs on novel tasks is attributable to a 'format gap'—sensitivity to linguistic instruction style—rather than a fundamental 'competence gap' in underlying visual reasoning and task planning.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework that generates synthetic, format-agnostic task specifications (e.g., programmatic descriptions, abstract scene graphs, or formal logic predicates) for a suite of visual reasoning and manipulation tasks. We will probe base VLMs (e.g., CLIP-based or DINOv2-based backbones) and their instruction-tuned variants (e.g., LLaVA, InstructBLIP) on these canonical specifications. Performance will be compared against a battery of linguistically varied natural language instructions for the same tasks, using controlled degradation analysis to isolate the contribution of format misalignment.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first diagnostic tool to quantitatively decompose VLM failures into format- vs. competence-based errors. It will yield concrete guidelines for when instruction tuning is necessary versus when more efficient pre-training on core competencies suffices, directly informing the design of training pipelines for robust, real-world robotic policies.
proposals.novelty: While prior work probes VLM robustness via linguistic paraphrasing, our approach is novel in constructing a canonical, non-linguistic task specification as a ground-truth probe for core competence, enabling a definitive decoupling not possible with natural language-only evaluations.

proposals.title: DyViC: Dynamic Visual Context for Temporally-Grounded Robot Instruction Following
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language models (VLMs) for robotics use static, uniform visual tokenization, which is inefficient and inflexible for long-horizon tasks requiring both global scene understanding and fine-grained local manipulation. This mismatch is acute in real-world human-robot collaboration, where users give sequential instructions and corrections that demand adaptive visual attention. A system that can dynamically reconfigure its visual parsing based on temporal task demands is crucial for scalable and natural embodied interaction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a VLM equipped with a dynamic, multi-resolution visual tokenizer, guided by a temporal reasoning module, will significantly outperform static models on long-horizon instruction-following and correction tasks by efficiently allocating visual computation to task-relevant regions over time.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel architecture integrating a dynamic vision encoder (e.g., a modified Vision Transformer with adaptive patch size control) with a temporal reasoning transformer. The system will process a stream of images, instructions, and corrections. A learned policy, conditioned on the language input and previous context, will output a spatial-temporal 'glimpse map' to select regions for high-resolution tokenization in each step. We will train this model on a synthetic dataset of multi-step manipulation tasks with procedural corrections, building on frameworks like ProVox for situated collaboration.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel VLM architecture with dynamic, task-driven visual representation, 2) a benchmark for evaluating multi-step, corrective instruction following in embodied settings, and 3) empirical evidence linking adaptive perception to long-horizon task performance, advancing towards cognitive paradigms for robot perception-reasoning interfaces.
proposals.novelty: While prior work explores multi-resolution vision or temporal reasoning in isolation, DyViC uniquely fuses them, enabling on-the-fly, language-conditioned adjustment of visual parsing resolution across a sequence of actions—a non-obvious capability critical for efficient real-world human-robot dialogue.

proposals.title: The Turing Test Pyramid for Embodied Agents: A Hierarchical Framework for Diagnosing Visual Reasoning and Planning Failures
proposals.motivation: Current evaluation of embodied AI agents, particularly Vision-Language Models (VLMs) for robot planning, relies on aggregate success rates on benchmarks like ALFRED or BEHAVIOR. This provides a coarse performance score but fails to diagnose the precise stage where reasoning breaks down—be it in visual perception, object affordance understanding, temporal sequencing, or handling novel compositions. A more granular, diagnostic evaluation framework is needed to guide progress beyond incremental benchmark gains.
proposals.hypothesis: Adapting the structured, hierarchical 'Turing Test Pyramid' from cognitive developmental science to embodied AI will enable fine-grained diagnosis of failure modes in visual reasoning and task planning, revealing that current models fail at specific, predictable levels of abstraction and compositional novelty rather than uniformly across tasks.
proposals.method: We will construct a hierarchical pyramid of visual planning tasks for tabletop manipulation, inspired by cognitive hierarchies. Each level increases in complexity (e.g., Level 1: single-object manipulation with direct instruction; Level 5: multi-step planning with unseen object combinations and implicit physical constraints). We will instantiate this pyramid in a simulated environment (e.g., PyBullet or SAPIEN) and develop a procedural generation framework to create novel task compositions. We will evaluate state-of-the-art policy learning methods (e.g., BC transformers) and VLMs used for planning (e.g., fine-tuned variants of Flamingo or GPT-4V) by analyzing their performance degradation across pyramid levels.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) A novel, open-source diagnostic benchmark for embodied reasoning that moves beyond monolithic scores. 2) A detailed failure mode analysis of current methods, pinpointing whether breakdowns occur in perception, reasoning, or generalization. 3) A formalized evaluation methodology that can guide the development of more robust, compositionally-generalizable policies.
proposals.novelty: While hierarchical task decomposition is common in robotics, applying a cognitive science-inspired, structured test pyramid specifically for fine-grained diagnosis of AI reasoning limits is novel. This approach systematically decouples and tests core competencies in a way that standard benchmarks do not.

proposals.title: REASON-VIS: A Benchmark for Compositional Visual Reasoning and Planning in Embodied Agents
proposals.motivation: Current evaluation suites for vision-language models (VLMs) and robotic policies focus on short-horizon tasks or single-step question answering, failing to capture the complex, multi-step reasoning required for real-world embodied interaction. As research moves towards scaling embodiment and proactive human-robot collaboration, there is a critical need for standardized benchmarks that stress-test compositional reasoning, visual planning, and long-horizon task understanding. This gap limits our ability to measure progress towards robust, generalizable agents.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that extending evaluation to complex, compositional visual reasoning tasks will reveal fundamental limitations in current VLM-based policies and provide a more accurate predictor of their real-world manipulation and collaboration performance.
proposals.method: We will design REASON-VIS, a benchmark built on simulated environments (e.g., AI2-THOR, Habitat) and real-world video datasets. It will feature tasks requiring: 1) **Visual Planning**: generating action sequences to achieve goals from visual scenes, 2) **Compositional QA**: answering questions that require chaining multiple visual and spatial inferences. We will adapt and evaluate a framework of state-of-the-art methods, including modular neuro-symbolic architectures and end-to-end VLM-based planners (e.g., extensions of ProVox), analyzing their performance breakdowns.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the community with a rigorous, open-source benchmark for complex embodied reasoning, enabling systematic comparison of algorithms. It will yield a detailed analysis of failure modes in current models, guiding future research in perception-reasoning interfaces and scalable policy learning for manipulation.
proposals.novelty: Unlike existing benchmarks, REASON-VIS explicitly integrates compositional reasoning with visual planning in situated, task-oriented contexts, creating a direct bridge between cognitive probing paradigms and embodied agent evaluation.

proposals.title: NICHE-VIS: A Functional Niche Framework for Synergistic Ensembles of Pretrained Visual Encoders in Embodied Reasoning
proposals.motivation: Current robot learning and embodied AI systems predominantly rely on single, general-purpose visual encoders like CLIP or DINOv2, which may lack the specialized capabilities needed for complex, multi-faceted tasks requiring geometric reasoning, fine-grained manipulation, or robust out-of-distribution perception. This monolithic approach mirrors a fragile ecosystem with low biodiversity. A systematic method to combine complementary visual representations, analogous to functional groups in ecology, could create more robust and capable perception systems for real-world robot tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: By defining and modeling the distinct functional 'niches' (e.g., semantic grounding, geometric invariance, texture modeling) of diverse pretrained visual encoders (CLIP, DINOv2, MAE, masked feature models), we can learn combination strategies that yield synergistic performance gains in embodied reasoning benchmarks, surpassing any single encoder or naive ensemble.
proposals.method: We will first characterize the functional niche of each encoder via a suite of probing tasks measuring semantic, geometric, and textural understanding. We will then develop a lightweight, task-adaptive gating network that dynamically combines encoder features based on the inferred demands of a given scene and downstream policy. This framework will be integrated into a manipulation policy learning setup (e.g., using BC or RL) and evaluated on benchmarks like RLBench and real-robot tasks from ProVox, measuring success rate and robustness to visual perturbations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a principled, ecology-inspired framework for analyzing and combining visual encoders, 2) a practical method for building more robust visual perception systems for robotics, and 3) new insights into the complementary strengths of modern self-supervised visual models, moving beyond leaderboard benchmarking toward engineered synergy.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the application of ecological niche theory as a formal lens for ensemble construction in computer vision, moving beyond performance-weighted averaging to a functional, task-aware combination strategy. This addresses a gap in both the visual representation and embodied AI communities, which have largely treated encoders in isolation.

proposals.title: Beyond CLIP and DINO: Evaluating Masked Autoencoder Representations for Visuomotor Policy Learning and Reasoning
proposals.motivation: Current robot learning and visual reasoning systems predominantly rely on visual representations from CLIP (contrastive) or DINOv2 (self-distillation). While effective, these models may not optimally capture the fine-grained spatial and physical properties critical for manipulation. Pre-2026, masked autoencoders (MAEs) and other feature prediction models have shown strong performance in dense prediction tasks but remain under-explored for embodied, task-oriented applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that visual representations from masked reconstruction objectives (e.g., MAE, iBOT) will provide superior sample efficiency and generalization for visuomotor policy learning and visual reasoning in manipulation tasks, compared to dominant contrastive and distillation-based encoders, by better preserving spatial and structural scene information.
proposals.method: We will conduct a systematic evaluation of pre-trained visual encoders (CLIP-ViT, DINOv2, MAE, and iBOT) within a unified robot learning framework. We will freeze each encoder and train lightweight policy heads (e.g., diffusion or MLP) on real-world manipulation benchmarks (e.g., RLBench, real robot data from ProVox). We will measure sample efficiency, success rates, and robustness to visual perturbations. Additionally, we will probe the representations on visual reasoning tasks relevant to human-robot collaboration.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive benchmark of MAE-style representations for robot learning, establishing clear trade-offs between representation families. It will deliver practical guidance for selecting visual backbones in embodied AI and may lead to new hybrid architectures tailored for physical interaction.
proposals.novelty: While MAEs are known in computer vision, their systematic evaluation and adaptation for closed-loop visuomotor control and embodied reasoning is novel. This bridges the gap between generic visual pre-training and the specific demands of robot task and policy learning.

proposals.title: MetaMixer: Online Meta-Optimization of Data Sampling for Task-Aware Visual-Language Policy Training
proposals.motivation: Training robust visual-language models (VLMs) for embodied tasks requires mixing diverse datasets (e.g., web images, simulation, real robot data). Pre-2026, mixing strategies are static or based on simple heuristics, ignoring dynamic training interference and the target task distribution. This leads to suboptimal policies that fail to generalize effectively in real-world manipulation and collaboration scenarios, a core challenge in Siddharth Karamcheti's prior work on robot language and reasoning.
proposals.hypothesis: A meta-optimized controller that dynamically adjusts data sampling based on real-time training metrics (e.g., gradient alignment, loss curvature) and a target task prior will yield visual-language policies with superior robustness and task-specific performance compared to static or curriculum-based mixing.
proposals.method: We will develop a meta-controller, modeled as a small neural network or differentiable optimizer, that takes as input online metrics of training dynamics (per-domain gradient norms, cosine similarity between batch gradients, loss curvature estimates) and a conditioning vector encoding the target task distribution (e.g., manipulation vs. navigation). This controller will be trained via a bi-level optimization loop to output sampling weights that maximize the expected validation performance on a held-out suite of target tasks, using a lightweight proxy like gradient descent on the validation loss. The base model will be a transformer-based VLM policy, trained on a multimodal pool including datasets like Something-Something, Ego4D, and RT-1.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a general framework for task-aware, dynamic data mixing for embodied AI training, 2) empirical evidence on which training dynamics metrics best predict interference and transfer, and 3) improved VLM policies for human-robot collaboration and manipulation, evaluated in simulated and real-world settings.
proposals.novelty: While meta-learning for hyperparameter optimization exists, applying online meta-optimization specifically to dynamic data sampling for visual-language policy training, conditioned on a downstream task distribution, is a novel integration of ideas from meta-learning, multi-task learning, and embodied AI.

proposals.title: Curriculum-VLM: Dynamic Data Scheduling for Efficient Embodied Reasoning
proposals.motivation: Training large visual-language models (VLMs) for embodied tasks like robot manipulation requires ingesting vast, heterogeneous datasets (e.g., image captions, video narrations, scene graphs). Current methods mix this data statically or with simple heuristics, ignoring the pedagogical principle that learning is most efficient when concepts are introduced in a structured, adaptive sequence. This leads to suboptimal sample efficiency and poor generalization on complex reasoning tasks, which is critical for real-world robot deployment where data collection is expensive.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a dynamic data scheduler, which sequences and mixes visual-language training data based on real-time model performance feedback—inspired by spiral curricula and mastery learning—will yield VLMs that achieve superior sample efficiency and final performance on downstream embodied reasoning benchmarks compared to standard static or random scheduling.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework that treats diverse datasets (e.g., CC3K, VQA, Ego4D, Something-Something) as curriculum modules. A lightweight scheduler network, trained via reinforcement learning with a reward based on validation loss on target tasks (e.g., ALFRED, VIMA), will select the next data batch. The state will include the model's recent performance profile across task categories. We will integrate this scheduler into the training loop of a VLM backbone (e.g., an OpenFlamingo variant) and evaluate on robot instruction-following and manipulation tasks in simulation and real user studies.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a generalizable framework for dynamic data scheduling in multimodal training, 2) empirical evidence on optimal data curricula for embodied VLM capabilities, and 3) more sample-efficient models that bridge visual-language understanding with actionable robot policies, advancing towards practical human-robot collaboration.
proposals.novelty: While curriculum learning exists in narrow domains, applying dynamic, feedback-driven scheduling to the heterogeneous, large-scale pretraining of VLMs for embodied reasoning is novel. The key innovation is the automated scheduler that learns an optimal data progression, moving beyond fixed hand-designed curricula.

proposals.title: DataMixRL: An Efficiency-Oriented Framework for Optimizing Visual-Language Data Composition in Embodied Policy Training
proposals.motivation: Training robust, generalizable policies for real-world robot manipulation requires massive, diverse datasets of visual-language-action trajectories. However, naively mixing data from different sources (e.g., simulation, real-world teleoperation, web videos) leads to inefficient training and negative interference, as evidenced by scaling law studies. A principled, automated strategy for optimal data mixing is critical for scaling embodied AI efficiently, especially given the high cost of collecting real robot data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a curriculum-based data mixing strategy, dynamically optimized via online performance metrics, can significantly improve the sample efficiency and final task performance of visual-language-action policy training compared to static or heuristic mixing schedules.
proposals.method: We will develop DataMixRL, a framework that treats data source selection as a multi-armed bandit problem. A lightweight controller network will observe the training state (e.g., loss curves, gradient norms) and predict a mixing ratio for different data sources (simulated, real manipulation, passive web videos). This ratio will be updated periodically to maximize a reward signal based on validation performance on a suite of held-out manipulation tasks. We will instantiate this within a transformer-based policy architecture (e.g., RT-2-style) and evaluate on benchmark tasks from Meta-World and real robot setups.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete, automated recipe for composing heterogeneous robot datasets, directly improving training efficiency. It will yield insights into the relative value of different data modalities at various training stages, contributing to a more scientific understanding of embodied scaling laws. The released framework will enable the community to train better policies with less data.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has studied scaling laws or used fixed curricula, dynamically optimizing data mixtures online based on live training signals for embodied policies is novel. This bridges the gap between high-level scaling analyses and low-level, practical training recipes.

proposals.title: Fusion Resilience: Analyzing and Improving Multimodal Integration for Robustness to Unreliable Language Corrections
proposals.motivation: Vision-language models (VLMs) are increasingly used for robot instruction and correction, but they assume language inputs are reliable. In real human-robot collaboration, corrections can be ambiguous, contradictory, or erroneous, leading to task failure. Understanding how different multimodal fusion architectures handle this linguistic noise is critical for deploying robust, trustworthy systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that late or gated fusion mechanisms will be more robust to unreliable language corrections than early fusion, and that explicitly modeling language confidence within the fusion process will significantly improve task performance under uncertainty.
proposals.method: We will conduct a systematic ablation study using a simulated tabletop manipulation benchmark. We will corrupt language corrections with semantic noise (ambiguity, contradiction) and evaluate standard fusion architectures (early, late, cross-attention, FiLM). Building on this, we will design and test a novel 'confidence-aware' fusion module that uses a lightweight auxiliary network to predict a reliability score for the language input, dynamically modulating its influence on the visual stream and policy output.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical taxonomy of fusion robustness for unreliable language in VLMs. It will yield a new fusion strategy that explicitly reasons about modality reliability, advancing the field beyond static fusion. The resulting framework will enable more resilient human-robot interaction where instructions are imperfect.
proposals.novelty: While prior work probes VLM reasoning, none systematically tests fusion architectures against *systematically perturbed* language corrections. The proposed confidence-aware fusion moves beyond fixed weighting to a dynamic, context-sensitive integration of modalities.

proposals.title: CURATE-VLM: Data Quality Filtering and Curriculum Learning for Efficient Vision-Language-Action Policy Training
proposals.motivation: Training Vision-Language Models (VLMs) for robot manipulation is bottlenecked by the scarcity and heterogeneity of high-quality, task-aligned data. While NLP has developed sophisticated data curation pipelines (e.g., quality filtering, deduplication, curriculum learning) crucial for scaling LLMs, these techniques are under-explored for embodied VLM training. Applying these methods could dramatically improve data efficiency and policy robustness in real-world manipulation tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: Systematically applying data curation and curriculum learning strategies from NLP to multimodal robot datasets will yield more data-efficient training and produce VLM-based policies with superior generalization and task success rates compared to training on unfiltered data.
proposals.method: We will develop CURATE-VLM, a framework that adapts NLP data curation to the embodied domain. First, we will design multimodal quality filters (e.g., CLIP-based caption-image alignment scores, action sequence feasibility checks) to clean datasets like BridgeV2 or RT-1. Second, we will implement a curriculum learning scheduler that sequences training data by estimated complexity, using metrics like task horizon length or object count. We will train VLM-based action prediction models (e.g., a Perceiver-Actor architecture) on both curated and baseline datasets, evaluating on a suite of real-world manipulation tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) A validated methodology for curating embodied VLM training data, 2) Empirical evidence on the impact of data quality vs. quantity in robot learning, and 3) Open-source tools and benchmarks for data curation in vision-language-action domains, advancing the field toward more systematic and scalable training paradigms.
proposals.novelty: While data curation is standard in NLP, its principled application to multimodal, action-oriented data for robot policy learning is novel. This work bridges the gap between large-scale static-data NLP techniques and the dynamic, sequential demands of embodied AI.

proposals.title: RobustVLM: Adversarial Training for Hallucination-Free Visual Language Models in Embodied Reasoning
proposals.motivation: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) are critical for high-level reasoning in embodied agents, but they are prone to hallucinating objects, attributes, and relations not present in a scene. This unreliability is a major barrier to deploying robots in open-world, human-centric environments where safety and trust depend on accurate perception. Pre-2026 methods for reducing hallucination, like instruction tuning, often fail under the distribution shifts and compositional complexity of real-world tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adversarial training and data augmentation techniques, which have proven effective for creating robust vision models against input perturbations, can be adapted to train VLMs that are robust to hallucination by learning to resist generating text that contradicts visual evidence.
proposals.method: We will adapt Projected Gradient Descent (PGD) and AutoAugment-style strategies to the VLM domain. For adversarial training, we will generate subtle, semantically meaningful adversarial image perturbations (e.g., occluding key objects, altering textures) paired with text queries designed to elicit common hallucinations. The model will be trained to maintain correct outputs under these attacks. Concurrently, we will develop a data augmentation pipeline that systematically creates hard negative examples by manipulating visual scenes (using tools like CARLA or Habitat) and generating contradictory captions to force fine-grained visual grounding.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel training framework for building more reliable, visually-grounded VLMs, directly improving the perceptual foundation for downstream robot policies. We will provide new benchmarks for evaluating VLM hallucination in embodied reasoning scenarios and demonstrate improved performance on tasks like instruction following and scene description in real-world manipulation experiments.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial robustness is well-studied in computer vision, its principled application to mitigate the distinct problem of VLM hallucination—a failure of text generation relative to visual input—is largely unexplored. This approach moves beyond post-hoc correction or dataset filtering to address the core robustness of the perception-reasoning interface.

proposals.title: Scaling Laws for Embodied Visual Language Models: The Impact of Data Diversity and Volume on Robotic Reasoning
proposals.motivation: Scaling laws for large language models (LLMs) are well-established, but their extension to embodied Visual Language Models (VLMs) for robotics remains an open question. Current VLM training for tasks like manipulation and human-robot collaboration is bottlenecked by relatively small, homogeneous datasets, limiting generalization and reasoning in complex, real-world environments. This work directly addresses the scaling hypothesis for embodied intelligence, a critical step toward robust, generalist robot policies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that scaling the diversity (modalities, tasks, environments) and volume of multimodal training data will yield predictable, power-law improvements in VLM performance on embodied reasoning benchmarks, with diversity being a more critical scaling factor than volume alone for out-of-distribution generalization.
proposals.method: We will construct a massive, diverse dataset by aggregating and unifying existing robot interaction datasets (e.g., RT-1, Open X-Embodiment), simulation episodes (e.g., from Isaac Gym), and internet-scale visual-language data, annotating all with structured task graphs. We will train a series of transformer-based VLMs (architecturally similar to Flamingo or PaLM-E) on controlled subsets, scaling data volume and diversity factors independently. Performance will be evaluated on a suite of real-robot manipulation tasks and the ProVox human-robot collaboration framework, measuring metrics like plan correctness, instruction following robustness, and success rate on novel objects and scenes.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first systematic study of scaling laws for embodied VLMs, quantifying the separate effects of data diversity and volume. It will release a large-scale, unified dataset and a set of scaled model checkpoints, providing a foundational resource for the field. The findings will guide efficient data collection strategies for future generalist robot learning.
proposals.novelty: While scaling laws are known for LLMs and pure vision models, applying this paradigm to the embodied, multi-modal setting—where data encompasses physical interactions, diverse visual perspectives, and language instructions—is novel. The explicit decoupling and study of diversity versus volume scaling for robotic reasoning extends beyond prior VLM scaling work focused primarily on web data.

proposals.title: REALM-Bench: A Benchmark for Robust Embodied Agent Learning in Multi-Stage, Open-World Manipulation
proposals.motivation: Current benchmarks for robot learning and vision-language-action models are often narrow, focusing on single skills in controlled environments. To achieve robust real-world utility, agents must be evaluated on diverse, multi-stage tasks that require complex reasoning, long-horizon planning, and adaptation to unstructured settings, a critical gap in pre-2026 evaluation practices. This work directly addresses the need for rigorous, scalable assessment of embodied intelligence as proposed in scaling law studies and cognitive paradigm probes.
proposals.hypothesis: That existing frameworks and policies will exhibit significant, systematic failures when evaluated on a broader spectrum of real-world tasks, revealing specific bottlenecks in visual reasoning, task decomposition, and long-horizon planning that are not apparent in current benchmarks.
proposals.method: We will develop REALM-Bench, a benchmark suite of 50+ physically-grounded, multi-stage manipulation tasks (e.g., 'prepare a simple meal' requiring object retrieval, tool use, and sequencing). We will instantiate these tasks in a real robot lab and a high-fidelity simulation (Isaac Gym). The evaluation pipeline from prior cognitive paradigm work will be extended to automatically score task success, efficiency, and the robustness of intermediate reasoning steps. We will conduct a large-scale study applying state-of-the-art methods (e.g., embodied VLMs, hierarchical policies, and proactive planners like ProVox) to this benchmark to perform a detailed failure mode analysis.
proposals.contribution: 1) A publicly available benchmark and evaluation protocol for complex, real-world embodied manipulation. 2) A comprehensive empirical analysis identifying the primary limitations of current methods in visual reasoning, planning, and execution. 3) Actionable insights to guide future research towards more robust and generalizable robot learning architectures.
proposals.novelty: While prior work creates frameworks or evaluates specific capabilities, this proposal systematically scales evaluation to a curated set of *diverse, integrative* real-world tasks, transforming a specific evaluation pipeline into a general tool for diagnosing embodied AI. It uniquely bridges the scale of embodied scaling laws with the granular failure analysis of cognitive paradigms.

ideas.title: Handle open-vocabulary object and scene terms
ideas.description: Generalize to novel objects and scenes not present in the training data using large-scale vision-language models.
ideas.sourceTitle: LILA: Language-Informed Latent Actions

ideas.title: Leverage foundation models for annotation
ideas.description: Use large vision-language models to automatically generate dense language descriptions or sub-task labels for trajectories.
ideas.sourceTitle: DROID: A Large-Scale In-The-Wild Robot Manipulation Dataset

ideas.title: Curriculum Visual-Language Model: Self-Supervised Visual Warm-Start for Efficient Joint Training
ideas.description: This system addresses the data inefficiency of pure joint-from-scratch training by implementing a structured two-phase curriculum. First, the visual encoder is trained from scratch using self-supervision on a large collection of unlabeled images, learning transferable visual features without text alignment. Second, this pre-trained but not frozen visual component is integrated with a language model, and the entire architecture is jointly fine-tuned on a curated vision-language dataset. This approach maintains the original goal of a unified, adaptable model without a fixed visual encoder, but achieves it with far less aligned data by ensuring the visual stream begins with a strong foundational understanding of images.

ideas.title: Benchmark on broader range of multimodal tasks
ideas.description: Evaluate models trained on interleaved data on a wider variety of multimodal reasoning and generation tasks.
ideas.sourceTitle: OBELISC: An Open Web-Scale Filtered Dataset of Interleaved Image-Text Documents

ideas.title: Optimize training efficiency and data mixing
ideas.description: Develop more efficient training recipes and optimal strategies for mixing diverse visual-language data.
ideas.sourceTitle: Prismatic VLMs: Investigating the Design Space of Visually-Conditioned Language Models

ideas.title: Adversarial Robustness for Emergent Affordance Learning in Foundation Models
ideas.description: This research develops security frameworks specifically for foundation models that learn object affordances from raw descriptions and interaction, rather than pre-defined annotations. The core challenge is to protect the emergent affordance learning process itself from adversarial attacks and data poisoning, which could cause the agent to learn dangerous or incorrect object uses. The synergy lies in creating adversarial training and verification protocols that use the model's own interactive exploration to detect and correct poisoned affordance inferences, thereby hardening the model's world understanding. This results in agents that are not only more flexible and general but also inherently more secure in their physical and semantic reasoning.

ideas.title: Challenge the need for separate visual encoder
ideas.description: Build a VLM from scratch without a frozen, pretrained visual encoder, learning visual features jointly.
ideas.sourceTitle: Prismatic VLMs: Investigating the Design Space of Visually-Conditioned Language Models

ideas.title: Contrastive Diffusion for Joint Relevance-Aware Visual Reconstruction
ideas.description: This research proposes to integrate a contrastive learning objective directly into the training of a diffusion-based visual reconstruction model. Instead of treating reconstruction and relevance classification as separate tasks, the model learns by contrasting positive pairs (e.g., an image and its relevant text/caption) against negative pairs during the diffusion denoising process. This forces the model's internal representations, shaped by the iterative generative refinement, to inherently encode semantic relevance, improving both the perceptual quality and the task-specific utility of the reconstructions. The synergy enables the generation of visually coherent outputs that are also optimally aligned with downstream relevance metrics, a capability neither contrastive classification nor standalone diffusion models achieve alone.

ideas.title: The 'Reference Genome' Protocol for VLM Pre-training
ideas.description: This idea inverts the training data priority assumption by drawing an analogy from genomics. In that field, the solution to handling noisy biological data was to first create a high-quality 'reference genome' as a foundational template. Adapting this, we propose a 'Reference Genome' Protocol for VLM Pre-training. The core is to prioritize the creation and intensive use of a small-scale, meticulously curated multi-modal 'reference corpus'—a set of perfectly aligned, richly annotated exemplars of core concepts and reasoning. The VLM is first pre-trained to convergence on this high-quality data to build robust, generalizable representations. This reference model then acts as a stable template or filter when subsequently training on orders-of-magnitude larger, noisier web-scraped datasets, guiding the alignment and integration of new information and preventing corruption of the core representations learned from the curated data.

ideas.title: Curriculum Learning and Dynamic Data Scheduling for Visual-Language Models
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from pedagogical science, where optimal learning outcomes are achieved through carefully sequenced curricula (spiral curriculum) and adaptive pacing (mastery learning), this research adapts these principles to optimize visual-language model training. The analogous solution involves structuring educational content from simple to complex and dynamically adjusting the mix based on learner assessment. Adapted to machine learning, the idea is to develop an automated, dynamic data scheduler that treats diverse visual-language datasets as educational modules. This scheduler would sequence and mix data—such as simple captions, complex scene graphs, video narratives, and noisy web data—based on real-time model performance feedback, creating an optimal, efficiency-focused training curriculum that evolves throughout the learning process.

ideas.title: A Theory of Emergent State Abstraction from Pixel-Based Foundation Models
ideas.description: This research direction develops a theoretical framework to explain the emergence of structured, abstract state representations in models trained at scale on raw pixel observations, such as videos or embodied agent streams. It seeks to identify the scaling laws, architectural components, and data properties that cause a transition from sub-symbolic perception to the discrete, relational, and compositional state spaces necessary for complex reasoning and planning. The goal is to provide a predictive theory for when and how foundation models processing pixels internally learn their own 'symbolic' abstractions, unifying the paradigms of subsymbolic learning and symbolic reasoning within a single scalable system.

ideas.title: Leverage large language models for caption generation
ideas.description: Use LLMs to automatically generate or augment training captions for human video datasets.
ideas.sourceTitle: Language-Driven Representation Learning for Robotics

ideas.title: Scale to multi-modal evidence selection
ideas.description: Select convincing evidence from images, text, and tables for visual question answering.
ideas.sourceTitle: Finding Generalizable Evidence by Learning to Convince Q&A Models

ideas.title: Inverse problem: data to task description
ideas.description: Develop models that watch robot interaction videos and infer the intended task goal or human instruction.
ideas.sourceTitle: DROID: A Large-Scale In-The-Wild Robot Manipulation Dataset

ideas.title: Causally Disentangled Language Grounding with Adversarial Robustness
ideas.description: This research develops a language grounding model that moves beyond simple correlation by enforcing causal structure and interpretability. The system uses an advanced language model to generate hypotheses but requires them to be validated through a disentangled visual-linguistic factor module that isolates attributes, relations, and actions. An adversarial training loop actively searches for linguistic rephrasings and visual confounders that break the model's correlations, using these failures to strengthen the causal model. This results in a robust grounding system whose decisions are traceable and less susceptible to spurious dataset patterns or linguistic tricks.

ideas.title: Action prediction requires end-to-end training
ideas.description: Decouple perception and action generation, using VLMs for planning and traditional controllers for execution.
ideas.sourceTitle: OpenVLA: An Open-Source Vision-Language-Action Model

ideas.title: Investigate more visual representation architectures
ideas.description: Explore beyond CLIP and DINOv2 to other pretrained visual encoders like MAE or masked feature prediction models.
ideas.sourceTitle: Prismatic VLMs: Investigating the Design Space of Visually-Conditioned Language Models

ideas.title: Crystallization Curriculum: Seeding Robust VLMs with Quality, then Scaling with Strategically Filtered Breadth
ideas.description: This approach begins VLM training exclusively on a small, meticulously curated 'seed' dataset to establish foundational reasoning, multimodal grounding, and safety principles. It then enters an expansion phase, where a learned routing network actively scores and selects batches from massive, noisy corpora based on their estimated utility for general knowledge and their compatibility with the seed's quality standards, dynamically blending data streams. The final curriculum phase fine-tunes the model on a high-quality mixture to reinforce alignment, resulting in a VLM that possesses both the robust reliability of curated data and the extensive world knowledge of web-scale training.

ideas.title: Narrow-Domain Multi-Modal Evidence Foundation Models
ideas.description: This research proposes training specialized foundation models on curated, narrow-domain datasets (e.g., medical imaging reports, scientific papers with figures, or engineering manuals) to become experts in a specific field. The core capability of these models is to perform multi-modal evidence selection, identifying and synthesizing the most convincing and relevant snippets of evidence from domain-specific text, images, and tables to answer complex visual questions. This moves beyond general-purpose VQA to produce answers backed by a curated, interpretable chain of domain-expert evidence, enabling high-stakes applications in diagnosis, technical analysis, and scholarly review where justification is as critical as the answer itself.

ideas.title: Use vision-language models as frozen teachers
ideas.description: Distill knowledge from large, pre-trained VLMs into a more robotics-specific representation model.
ideas.sourceTitle: Language-Driven Representation Learning for Robotics

ideas.title: Scale up training data diversity and volume
ideas.description: Train VLMs on orders-of-magnitude larger and more diverse multimodal datasets to test scaling laws.
ideas.sourceTitle: Prismatic VLMs: Investigating the Design Space of Visually-Conditioned Language Models

ideas.title: Learn from raw pixel observations, not symbolic states
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that symbolic state abstraction is necessary for efficient lifelong learning (ELLA). Drawing an analogy from computational neuroscience and visual cortex research, where the brain processes raw retinal input through hierarchical cortical layers (V1→V2→V4→IT) that progressively extract more complex features without symbolic abstraction, we propose adapting this biological solution to machine learning. The adapted idea involves designing a neural architecture with multiple processing layers that learn to extract increasingly abstract state representations directly from raw pixel observations through hierarchical feature learning. This approach would enable the ELLA framework to operate on learned, high-dimensional visual representations rather than requiring hand-engineered or pre-defined symbolic states, potentially improving the system's ability to learn continuously from raw visual inputs in complex environments.

ideas.title: Curriculum-Guided Adversarial Pair Refinement for Robust Contrastive Relevance Learning
ideas.description: This system overcomes the critical dependency on noisy pair construction in standard contrastive relevance learning. It implements a training curriculum that begins with high-confidence, clearly relevant and non-relevant document-query pairs, progressively incorporating more nuanced and harder examples. An integrated adversarial module continuously evaluates and refines the training pairs, down-weighting potentially mislabeled data. The final model produces multi-aspect representations, enabling a more robust and granular assessment of document relevance that correlates better with human judgments than classifiers trained on raw contrastive losses.

ideas.title: Extend evaluation suite to more complex tasks
ideas.description: Expand the standardized evaluation suite to include more complex reasoning tasks like visual planning or compositional question answering.
ideas.sourceTitle: Prismatic VLMs: Investigating the Design Space of Visually-Conditioned Language Models

ideas.title: Learn from visual observations via self-supervised temporal abstraction
ideas.description: This approach first trains a recurrent latent dynamics model on raw pixel observations using a self-supervised objective that enforces temporal coherence and predictability. The resulting latent state representation captures the essential, low-dimensional factors of the environment. The ELLA framework is then applied to this learned latent space, not the raw pixels, to efficiently acquire and compose skills. This hybrid method combines the representational power of deep learning for perception with the sample-efficient skill composition of ELLA, overcoming the fatal sample inefficiency of pure pixel-based learning.

ideas.title: Learn from synthetic video and captions
ideas.description: Train the representation entirely on procedurally generated simulation data with perfect captions.
ideas.sourceTitle: Language-Driven Representation Learning for Robotics

ideas.title: Transfer robust training from adversarial ML
ideas.description: Use adversarial training and data augmentation methods from robust vision models to reduce VLM hallucination.
ideas.sourceTitle: Prismatic VLMs: Investigating the Design Space of Visually-Conditioned Language Models

ideas.title: Apply transfer learning from web video
ideas.description: Pre-train visual representations on large-scale web video data before fine-tuning on the DROID robot dataset.
ideas.sourceTitle: DROID: A Large-Scale In-The-Wild Robot Manipulation Dataset

ideas.title: Invert the training data priority assumption
ideas.description: Prioritize high-quality, small-scale curated data over massive, noisy web-scraped datasets for VLM training.
ideas.sourceTitle: Prismatic VLMs: Investigating the Design Space of Visually-Conditioned Language Models

ideas.title: Diverse Instruction Abduction: Modeling the Plausible Instruction Manifold from Agent Behavior
ideas.description: This approach reframes the inverse problem as one of manifold learning, aiming to capture the entire set of language instructions that could explain an observed agent trajectory. Instead of a classifier producing one output, a generative model is trained to sample multiple, semantically distinct instructions that are all consistent with the behavior. The system incorporates a consistency verifier to ensure sampled instructions are valid and a diversity-promoting objective to cover the space of plausible explanations. This allows for robust interpretation of agent behavior, hypothesis generation, and the creation of richer training data for instruction-following models.

ideas.title: Transfer curriculum learning for abstraction
ideas.description: Use curriculum learning to gradually introduce commands of varying granularity to improve model training.
ideas.sourceTitle: Accurately and Efficiently Interpreting Human-Robot Instructions of Varying Granularities

ideas.title: Use contrastive learning for language grounding
ideas.description: Employ contrastive learning techniques to better align language embeddings with the geometry of the latent action space.
ideas.sourceTitle: LILA: Language-Informed Latent Actions

ideas.title: Train larger models on the full dataset
ideas.description: Train models with more parameters on the complete OBELICS dataset to explore scaling laws.
ideas.sourceTitle: OBELISC: An Open Web-Scale Filtered Dataset of Interleaved Image-Text Documents

ideas.title: Dynamic, Task-Aware Data Mixing via Online Meta-Optimization
ideas.description: This research develops a system for adaptive visual-language training that moves beyond static recipes. It employs a real-time controller that monitors training dynamics, such as gradient coherence and per-domain loss curvature, to meta-optimize data selection. The system dynamically adjusts sampling strategies to minimize interference and maximize positive transfer, conditioned on a target task distribution. This results in a training process that automatically tailors its data mix to both the model's instantaneous learning state and the intended downstream application.

ideas.title: Decoupling Instruction Format from Task Competence: A Diagnostic Framework for VLMs
ideas.description: This research proposes a diagnostic framework to rigorously test whether instruction tuning is necessary for learning underlying tasks or merely for aligning with human query formats. It generates synthetic, format-agnostic task specifications (e.g., via programmatic or abstract graph-based descriptions) to probe a base VLM's core capabilities independently of linguistic instruction style. By comparing performance degradation when moving from these canonical specifications to varied natural language instructions, the system can isolate the 'format gap' from the 'competence gap'. This allows for a definitive assessment of what instruction tuning truly provides, guiding more efficient model training paradigms.

ideas.title: Dynamic Visual Context for Multi-Step Instruction and Correction
ideas.description: This research direction develops a vision-language model that integrates dynamic, multi-resolution visual tokenization with the ability to process multi-step language instructions and corrections. The system can natively process images of any size or aspect ratio and, crucially, can adjust its visual parsing resolution on-the-fly based on the demands of a sequential task. For example, when instructed to 'first sketch a rough outline here, then later add fine details to the corner,' the model can initially use a coarse visual representation for the layout phase and then selectively apply high-resolution tokenization to the specified region for the detailing phase, all while maintaining coherence across the temporal instruction stream.

ideas.title: Scalable Neuro-Symbolic Pretraining for Emergent Compositional Reasoning
ideas.description: This research direction proposes to scale up neuro-symbolic instruction decomposition by integrating it directly into the pretraining objective of a large language model. Instead of applying neuro-symbolic methods only during fine-tuning, the model is pretrained on an ultra-diverse corpus that includes explicit representations of task decompositions, logical constraints, and sub-goal hierarchies. The scaled data enables the neuro-symbolic module to learn a broad, cross-domain library of compositional primitives. Crucially, the model then uses this learned decomposition capability to generate novel, valid, and complex compositional training examples, creating a virtuous cycle that continuously improves both its data diversity and its intrinsic capacity for structured reasoning.

ideas.title: Extend evaluation to more diverse real-world tasks
ideas.description: Apply the rigorous evaluation pipeline to a broader and more complex set of real-world manipulation tasks.
ideas.sourceTitle: A Careful Examination of Large Behavior Models for Multitask Dexterous Manipulation

ideas.title: Incorporate data curation techniques from NLP
ideas.description: Apply data quality filtering and curriculum learning strategies from large language model training to VLM data.
ideas.sourceTitle: Prismatic VLMs: Investigating the Design Space of Visually-Conditioned Language Models

ideas.title: Exploring Ensemble and Hybrid Visual Encoders via Ecological Niche Theory
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from community ecology, where ecosystem function is understood through complementary functional groups rather than single species, this research adapts the solution to computer vision. The analogous ecological solution involves modeling how different species occupy distinct niches to contribute complementary traits to system resilience. Adapted to the original seed idea of exploring beyond CLIP and DINOv2, this proposes a systematic investigation into constructing ensembles or hybrid architectures from diverse pretrained visual encoders (including MAE, masked feature models, and others). The core adapted idea is to develop a framework that defines the functional 'niche'—such as semantic understanding, geometric invariance, or texture modeling—of each encoder type and learns optimal combination strategies, moving beyond benchmarking individual models toward engineering synergistic visual representation systems.

ideas.title: Capability Emergence via Compositional Language-Grounded Modularity
ideas.description: This research direction investigates a hybrid architecture where a central, moderately-sized language model (the 'grounding model') is trained not for direct task execution, but for understanding instructions and dynamically assembling workflows from a fixed library of smaller, pre-trained functional modules (e.g., classifiers, code generators, reasoners). The key challenge is developing training methods for the grounding model to learn compositional reasoning—correlating instructions with novel, efficient combinations of modules rather than retrieving single solutions. This approach decouples capability breadth from parameter count, as new tasks can be addressed by recombining existing modules, fundamentally challenging the paradigm that scale is the primary path to generalization.

ideas.title: Scale task diversity in evaluation suite
ideas.description: Greatly expand the unified evaluation platform to include dozens of distinct robot learning problems.
ideas.sourceTitle: Language-Driven Representation Learning for Robotics

ideas.title: Adapting the 'Turing Test Pyramid' from Cognitive Science for AI Evaluation
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from cognitive developmental science, which uses structured hierarchies like the 'Turing Test Pyramid' to assess human reasoning capabilities, this idea proposes to adapt that methodology for AI evaluation. The analogous solution involves creating graded, multi-level test batteries that pinpoint the precise stage of competence. Adapted to AI research, this means expanding the evaluation suite by designing a hierarchical pyramid of complex reasoning tasks (e.g., in visual planning or compositional QA). Each level of the pyramid would introduce greater complexity, abstraction, or novel composition, allowing researchers to systematically diagnose the boundaries of a model's reasoning, identifying the specific point and nature of failure rather than providing a single aggregate score.

ideas.title: Robust Multimodal Fusion Under Ambiguous or Contradictory Language Corrections
ideas.description: This research investigates how different cross-attention and fusion mechanisms for vision-language models behave when the language input is an ambiguous, contradictory, or erroneous natural language correction. We conduct a detailed ablation study where the 'language' branch is systematically perturbed with unreliable corrections, measuring the robustness of various fusion architectures (e.g., early, late, hybrid fusion) in filtering noise and preventing error propagation. The goal is to identify which fusion mechanisms are most resilient to linguistic uncertainty and to develop new fusion strategies that can explicitly model the confidence or reliability of the language modality, moving beyond the assumption of perfectly accurate instructions.

ideas.title: Multimodal Training Data Inference: Auditory and Visual Membership Inference Attacks on Foundation Models
ideas.description: This research investigates the feasibility of inferring or extracting the private, multimodal training data (e.g., audio clips, spoken phrases, or associated images) from a released multimodal foundation model. It explores whether the complex, cross-modal representations learned by such models create new, stronger channels for data leakage compared to unimodal models, or if the disentanglement of modalities offers inherent privacy benefits. The work will develop novel membership inference and data reconstruction attacks tailored to the multimodal setting and propose corresponding defense mechanisms that protect sensitive training data across all integrated sensory streams without crippling model utility.

ideas.title: Crowdsourced Curriculum Bootstrapping for Instruction Tuning
ideas.description: This research proposes a system that integrates curriculum learning principles directly into the crowdsourcing pipeline for generating and validating instruction-following data. Instead of a static task list, the system presents annotators with a dynamically sequenced curriculum of instruction-complexity tasks, starting simple. Their performance and the consensus metrics on these tasks are used not only to validate data but also to assess and train the annotators themselves, promoting a 'bootstrapping' effect. This creates a synergistic feedback loop where high-consensus outputs from earlier, simpler tasks inform the construction and quality thresholds for more complex tasks, leading to a scalable protocol that yields progressively higher-quality, complexity-grounded training data for language models.

ideas.title: Large-scale pre-training for correction understanding
ideas.description: Pre-train the language and control model on vast datasets of simulated corrections for broad skill acquisition.
ideas.sourceTitle: No, to the Right: Online Language Corrections for Robotic Manipulation via Shared Autonomy

ideas.title: Scaling Laws for Emergent Cross-Modal Grounding
ideas.description: This research direction proposes to scale both the diversity of integrated sensory modalities and the volume/diversity of training data in a co-designed experiment. The core hypothesis is that by training on orders-of-magnitude larger datasets that pair core modalities (vision, language) with many auxiliary, often sparse modalities (e.g., haptic feedback, spectral data, non-speech audio, sensorimotor streams), new scaling laws will emerge that govern a model's ability to perform true cross-modal grounding and inference. The goal is not merely to add inputs but to discover the data and architectural scaling required for a system to develop a unified, amodal understanding of concepts that can be accessed and reasoned through any constituent sensory channel.
