ideas.title: Adaptive Task Progression via Cognitive Load Theory and Spaced Repetition
ideas.description: This idea adapts solutions from Educational Psychology and Instructional Design to the problem of structuring task progression for instruction-following models. The analogous field solves complex skill acquisition by sequencing sub-skills while actively managing the learner's cognitive load, using techniques like interleaving and spaced repetition to reinforce mastery. The adapted research idea is to create a dynamic curriculum learning framework that explicitly models a proxy for the model's cognitive load based on performance metrics. Progression through instruction complexity would not be linear but adaptive, pausing or revisiting simpler variants of challenging instructions at algorithmically determined intervals inspired by spaced repetition systems. This aims to optimize long-term retention of complex instruction-following capabilities and prevent catastrophic forgetting of simpler skills.

ideas.title: A Causal and Predictive Theory of Emergence: Integrating Multi-Scale Experimentation with Mechanistic Modeling
ideas.description: This research program develops a falsifiable, predictive theory for emergent capabilities by tightly integrating controlled experimentation with formal modeling. It establishes a standardized experimental framework using diverse model 'organisms'—systematically varying scale, architecture, and data—to isolate the causal drivers of emergence beyond mere correlation with parameter count. The resulting theoretical models are grounded in empirical scaling laws, formal task complexity measures, and mechanistic interpretability, enabling the prediction of capability thresholds and the design of models with targeted emergent behaviors.

ideas.title: A Federated Training Protocol with Proof-of-Contribution Consensus for Foundation Models
ideas.description: This idea inverts the centralization of model development by drawing an analogy from decentralized network infrastructure. In fields like cryptocurrency mining, a decentralized set of actors is coordinated via consensus protocols and cryptographic incentives to build and maintain a public good (the blockchain). The analogous solution is a tokenized incentive layer atop a federated learning framework. The adapted research idea is to design a federated training protocol for foundation models that uses a 'Proof-of-Contribution' consensus mechanism. This system would cryptographically verify and reward compute, data, or algorithmic contributions from distributed participants, using a transparent ledger to manage model updates and access rights. This creates a sustainable, incentive-aligned, and truly decentralized alternative to centralized corporate or institutional control over foundation model development.

ideas.title: Applying Immunological Memory to Mitigate Bias Propagation
ideas.description: This idea expands the original epidemiological model by drawing an analogy from immunology. The analogous problem is preventing the spread of infectious disease, solved through vaccination which primes the immune system's memory. The adapted research idea applies this solution to AI bias by proposing the development of 'bias vaccines.' Before a base model is released for downstream use, it would undergo a 'vaccination' phase involving exposure to de-biased or counterfactual data that inoculates it against known bias 'pathogens.' This aims to create a persistent, adaptive resistance within the model, reducing the rate and severity of bias propagation when the model is later fine-tuned or adapted, similar to how immunological memory provides long-term protection against disease.

ideas.title: Runtime Security Contracts and Ecosystem Monitoring for Foundation Model Integrity
ideas.description: This research develops a dynamic security framework centered on formal, executable 'security contracts' that travel with a foundation model, specifying its guaranteed properties and operational boundaries. A lightweight monitoring and attestation layer is designed to enforce these contracts in real-time across diverse deployment environments, from cloud APIs to downstream integrated applications. The system continuously validates model behavior against known and emergent attack patterns, using adversarial simulation and anomaly detection to identify breaches of the contract. This enables automated mitigation, such as input filtering or model rollback, and provides auditable evidence of integrity failures, moving security from a static pre-release checklist to a continuous, adaptive process.

ideas.title: Adaptive Communication Protocols for Multi-Agent ELLA via Swarm Consensus
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by using an analogy from distributed systems and swarm robotics. The analogous problem is how decentralized agents with limited communication bandwidth achieve robust collective intelligence and task completion, solved through stigmergic signaling and consensus protocols. Adapted to the ELLA context, the research would develop adaptive communication protocols for multi-agent or human-in-the-loop settings. Multiple ELLA instances (or human partners) would engage in a decentralized negotiation process to interpret language instructions, decomposing tasks and assigning roles based on local expertise and confidence. The system would mimic swarm consensus by using minimal, structured signals to build a shared understanding, enabling collaborative task execution where the communication strategy itself is learned and optimized for efficiency and robustness, moving beyond a single agent simply following instructions to a collective solving them.

ideas.title: Algorithmic Thermodynamics: Exploiting Computational State Transitions for Efficiency
ideas.description: This idea adapts principles from thermodynamics and heat engine design to algorithmic improvement. In thermodynamics, practical efficiency gains came from designing multi-stage processes that exploit state changes (like vaporization) within fundamental limits, rather than just optimizing a single stage. The analogous solution is to model computational processes as having distinct 'states'—such as high-entropy (broad, probabilistic search) and low-entropy (focused, deterministic calculation). The adapted research idea is to develop a formal 'Algorithmic Thermodynamics' framework that decomposes problems into an optimal sequence of these computational states. The goal is to design algorithms that strategically induce transitions between states to maximize the useful 'work' (progress toward a solution) extracted per unit of computational effort (FLOP), thereby reducing total compute required for a given capability, much like an engineered heat cycle improves upon a naive boiler.

ideas.title: Applying Epidemiological Causal Inference to Measure AI Deployment's Societal Impact
ideas.description: This idea adapts causal inference methodologies from epidemiology to the problem of quantifying the societal impact of AI deployment. In epidemiology, researchers face the challenge of estimating the causal effect of an intervention (like a vaccine rollout) from complex, observational population data, using rigorous techniques to control for confounders and establish counterfactuals. By treating regions, industries, or firms with varying levels and timing of AI adoption as the 'exposed' and 'control' groups, we can apply analogous methods—such as difference-in-differences, propensity score matching, and instrumental variable approaches (using, for example, variation in broadband infrastructure as an instrument)—to isolate the causal impact of deployment on key economic and labor market outcomes. This provides a more robust, attribution-focused measurement framework than standard econometric correlations.

ideas.title: Co-Design of Formal Specifications and Verified Safety Guarantees via Iterative Refinement
ideas.description: This approach creates a feedback-driven framework that treats the formal safety specification itself as a learned, evolving component. The system proposes an initial set of formal properties, then deploys the model (or a proxy) in a constrained, monitored environment. Discrepancies between human reviewer judgments and the formal property evaluations are flagged as specification candidates for refinement or correction. This iterative process of formalization, testing, and human-guided revision continues until the specifications stabilize and are validated as correctly capturing the intended safety goals, at which point full formal verification of the model against those stabilized specs provides robust guarantees.

ideas.title: Adapt the International Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) model for foundation model governance
ideas.description: This research proposes adapting the governance model of the International Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) to the challenge of governing foundation models. The analogous problem in climate science was the need for a trusted, interdisciplinary scientific body to synthesize complex, contested knowledge for global policymakers. The solution was the IPCC, which assesses and reports on climate science without dictating policy. Adapted to foundation models, the idea is to establish a similar international, interdisciplinary panel comprising experts from computer science, law, ethics, economics, and social sciences. This panel would be tasked with producing regular, comprehensive assessments on the capabilities, risks, and societal impacts of foundation models. Its consensus-driven reports would provide a crucial, neutral evidence base to inform national regulations, international agreements, industry standards, and public debate, thereby enabling more informed and coordinated governance for this transformative technology.

ideas.title: Architecting Enforceable Policy Modules for Technically-Grounded Foundation Model Governance
ideas.description: This research designs and prototypes a technical architecture where interdisciplinary governance principles are not just discussed but encoded. It develops a framework for translating legal and ethical rules (e.g., fairness constraints, disclosure requirements, use prohibitions) into verifiable, machine-readable policy modules. These modules are then integrated into the model development stack, enabling automated compliance testing, runtime monitoring, and audit trails. The outcome is a governance system that moves from abstract deliberation to concrete, technical enforcement, closing the gap between policy aspiration and operational reality.

ideas.title: Apply Formal Verification with Safety-Critical System Certification Frameworks
ideas.description: This idea adapts the process-based certification framework from safety-critical aerospace software (DO-178C) to AI model safety. In avionics, certification relies not just on final product testing but on rigorous, evidence-generating processes throughout development. The adapted research proposes creating a 'Model Development Assurance Level' (M-DAL) framework for AI. This framework would define graded levels of safety criticality, each requiring specific, formally verifiable processes in data handling, architectural design, training, and output validation. Formal verification methods would then be integrated to prove core safety properties (e.g., bounded output, absence of certain failure modes), providing the conclusive evidence needed for certification within this structured, process-oriented safety regime, moving beyond one-off verification to a holistic, certifiable development lifecycle.

ideas.title: Socio-Technical Agent-Based Epidemiology for Bias Propagation
ideas.description: This research develops a hybrid modeling framework that combines core epidemiological concepts with an agent-based model of downstream developers and systems. Agents are endowed with traits like bias detection capability, ethical guidelines, and performance objectives, which influence their decisions when encountering potentially biased components. The simulation tracks not just the spread of a defect but its transformation, allowing for the study of how community norms, tooling, and incentive structures can be designed to promote bias mitigation rather than passive propagation. This moves the model from a simple contagion analogy to a testbed for intervention strategies in complex, adaptive development ecosystems.

ideas.title: Distributional Causal Econometrics for Equity-Aware Impact Assessment
ideas.description: This research employs high-resolution microdata and advanced causal inference methods to measure the distributional economic impacts of technological deployment. It moves beyond aggregate labor market metrics to quantify heterogeneous effects across worker subgroups, occupations, and geographies, explicitly identifying winners and losers. The approach integrates welfare economics to assess impacts on inequality and economic security, providing a robust, equity-aware evaluation of societal impact that informs targeted policy design.
